topics.title: Introduction to Nutraceuticals and Functional Foods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT863 - NUTRACEUTICALS AND FUNCTIONAL FOODS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Nutraceuticals and Functional Foods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Nutraceuticals

The term **Nutraceutical** is a portmanteau (a linguistic blend) of two words: **"Nutrition"** and **"Pharmaceutical."** It was formally coined by **Dr. Stephen DeFelice**, Founder and Chairman of the Foundation for Innovation in Medicine (FIM), Cranford, NJ, USA, in his landmark **1989** paper published in the journal *Nutrition Reviews*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (DeFelice, 1989):**
> A *nutraceutical* is any substance that is a food or a part of a food and provides medical or health benefits, including the **prevention** and **treatment** of disease. Such products may range from isolated nutrients, dietary supplements, and herbal products to processed foods such as cereals, soups, and beverages.

Mathematically expressed as the intersection of nutrition and pharmaceutical domains:

$$
\text{Nutraceutical} = \text{Nutrition} \cap \text{Pharmaceutical}
$$

### 1.2 Formal Definition of Functional Foods

The concept of **Functional Foods** originated in **Japan in the 1980s**, where the Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare coined the term **Foods for Specified Health Use (FOSHU)** in **1991** to regulate foods that possess physiological benefits beyond basic nutritional functions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition of Functional Food:**
> A functional food is a conventional or modified food that, in addition to providing nutrients and energy, contains **bioactive compounds** in sufficient quantities to produce a measurable health-promoting, disease-preventing, or performance-enhancing benefit when consumed as part of a normal diet.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **spectrum (continuum)** rather than a binary category:

| Stage | Real-World Analogy | Health Effect |
|---|---|---|
| 🍎 **Ordinary Food** (e.g., raw apple) | Fuel for daily life | Basic nutrition (energy, macro/micronutrients) |
| 🥛 **Functional Food** (e.g., probiotic yogurt, omega-3 fortified milk) | Fuel + a built-in "mechanic" | Basic nutrition + improved physiological function (gut health, heart) |
| 💊 **Nutraceutical** (e.g., fish oil capsule, garlic extract tablet) | Targeted "biological tool" | Concentrated bioactive dose; risk reduction/therapy support |
| 🏥 **Pharmaceutical Drug** (e.g., Atorvastatin) | Prescription "intervention" | Specific disease treatment under medical supervision |

> [!TIP]
> **Student Mnemonic (KTU Board Friendly):**
> **"FFNP Spectrum"** — **F**ood → **F**unctional Food → **N**utraceutical → **P**harmaceutical. Each stage increases in **potency, isolation, regulatory control**, and **therapeutic specificity**.

### 1.4 Related Standardized Terminology (Syllabus Highlights)

- **Phytochemicals** — Bioactive compounds derived from plant sources (e.g., polyphenols, carotenoids).
- **Zoochemicals** — Bioactive compounds derived from animal sources (e.g., omega-3 fatty acids, CLA).
- **FFHP** — Food For Health Promotion; a working category covering the entire functional food domain.
- **Designer Foods** — Foods purposely formulated with enhanced bioactive content.
- **Medical Foods** — Formulated to be consumed under physician supervision for specific disease management.
- **Dietary Supplements** — Concentrated sources of nutrients marketed in dose form (capsule, tablet, liquid).
- **Prebiotics & Probiotics** — Functional ingredients targeting the gut microbiome.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note on Terminology:**
> The Indian regulatory body **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India, 2006 Act)** treats "nutraceuticals" under the **Food Safety and Standards (Food or Health Supplements, Nutraceuticals, Foods for Special Dietary Use, Foods for Special Medical Purposes, Functional Foods, and Novel Foods) Regulations, 2016** — collectively termed **FSSAI-NF*. Students must be familiar with this regulatory umbrella for KTU viva and ESE questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Historical Evolution (Board Frequently Asked Timeline)

1. **1970s — Japan:** Concept of *Kinousei Shokuhin* (functional foods) emerges due to rising healthcare costs and an aging population.
2. **1984–1989 — Origin of "Nutraceutical":** DeFelice's hypothesis and formal coining of the word.
3. **1991 — Japan FOSHU:** First formal regulatory framework for functional foods established.
4. **1994 — DSHEA (USA):** Dietary Supplement Health and Education Act defines supplements (not drugs) for the U.S. market.
5. **2002 — European PASSCLAIM:** Process for the Assessment of Scientific Support for Claims on Foods consensus document.
6. **2006 — Indian FSSAI Act:** Foundation laid for unified food regulation in India.
7. **2016 — FSSAI-NF Regulations:** Operationalized the eight functional product categories in India.

### 2.2 Theoretical Basis: The Food–Drug Continuum

A **continuum model** underpins modern nutraceutical science. Every ingested substance can be positioned along an axis based on three parameters:

- **Concentration (C):** Dose of bioactive compound delivered.
- **Specificity (S):** Targeted physiological action.
- **Regulation (R):** Degree of statutory oversight.

$$
\text{Score}_{\text{FNP}} = w_1 \cdot C + w_2 \cdot S + w_3 \cdot R
$$

Where $w_1, w_2, w_3$ are **weighting coefficients** reflecting national regulatory priority, and $C, S, R \in [0, 10]$ on standardized scales. Higher scores indicate a substance that is more pharmaceutical in nature.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter / Concept | Symbol / Notation | Description / Unit | KTU Exam Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Nutraceutical | $\mathcal{N}$ | Food-derived substance with health benefit | Direct definition (3 marks) |
| Functional Food | $\mathcal{F}$ | Food providing benefit beyond nutrition | Direct definition (3 marks) |
| FOSHU | — | Foods for Specified Health Use (Japan, 1991) | History question |
| DeFelice Year | — | **1989** | Frequently asked year |
| FSSAI Year | — | **2006** (Act) and **2016** (NF Regulations) | Indian regulation |
| FFHP | — | Food For Health Promotion | Glossary item |
| Concentration Axis | $C$ | $\text{mg} \cdot \text{g}^{-1}$ of bioactive | Continuum theory |
| Daily Reference Value | DRV | Nutrient intake reference (e.g., $\approx 2\,\text{g}$ EPA+DHA) | Dietary recommendation |
| ORAC | — | Oxygen Radical Absorbance Capacity ($\mu\text{mol TE}/\text{g}$) | Antioxidant metric |
| Bioavailability | $F$ | Fraction absorbed (dimensionless, $0 < F \leq 1$) | Pharmacokinetics |
| No Observed Adverse Effect Level | NOAEL | $\text{mg} \cdot \text{kg}^{-1} \cdot \text{day}^{-1}$ | Toxicity threshold |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Remember that $\text{FOSHU} \rightarrow \text{Japan}$ and $\text{DeFelice} \rightarrow \text{USA}$ — this is a commonly confused pair in viva questions.

### 2.4 Classification of Nutraceuticals (Three Major Schemes)

#### Scheme A — Based on **Source / Origin** (Most Frequently Asked)

1. **Plant-derived Nutraceuticals (Phytochemicals)**
   - Carotenoids, Flavonoids, Phenolic Acids, Glucosinolates, Saponins, Phytoestrogens, Tannins, Capsaicinoids.
2. **Animal-derived Nutraceuticals (Zoochemicals)**
   - Omega-3 PUFA, CLA (Conjugated Linoleic Acid), Chondroitin, Glucosamine, Lecithin.
3. **Microbial-derived Nutraceuticals (Probiotics, Prebiotics, Synbiotics)**
   - *Lactobacillus*, *Bifidobacterium* species; FOS, Inulin, Beta-glucan.

#### Scheme B — Based on **Chemical Nature** (Most Scientifically Detailed)

| Class | Representative Compounds | Sources |
|---|---|---|
| Carotenoids | $\beta$-carotene, Lycopene, Lutein | Carrot, tomato, spinach |
| Polyphenols / Flavonoids | Quercetin, Catechin, Anthocyanins | Tea, berries, cocoa |
| Isothiocyanates | Sulforaphane | Broccoli, cabbage |
| Glucosinolates | Indole-3-carbinol | Cruciferous vegetables |
| Saponins | Ginsenosides | Ginseng |
| Phytoestrogens | Genistein, Daidzein | Soy |
| Fatty Acids | EPA, DHA, ALA | Fish oil, flaxseed |
| Probiotics | *L. acidophilus* | Yogurt, kefir |
| Prebiotics | FOS, Inulin | Chicory, garlic, onion |

#### Scheme C — Based on **Regulatory / Therapeutic State**

- **Established Nutraceuticals:** With proven health claims (e.g., calcium + Vitamin D for osteoporosis).
- **Accepted Nutraceuticals:** With reasonable evidence.
- **Proposed Nutraceuticals:** Under investigation.
- **Disputed / Failed Nutraceuticals:** Where trials did not confirm benefit (e.g., high-dose $\beta$-carotene in smokers).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering / Industrial Utility

- **Food Engineering:** Encapsulation, microencapsulation, nanoemulsion, and spray-drying technologies to deliver bioactives without losing potency.
- **Pharmaceutical Engineering:** Bridging gap between food and pharma — lipid-based delivery systems, controlled-release matrices.
- **Analytical Chemistry:** HPLC, GC-MS, LC-MS used to quantify bioactives.
- **Biotechnology:** Fermentation processes to produce microbial-derived nutraceuticals (e.g., beta-carotene from *Blakeslea trispora*).
- **Quality Assurance:** Standardization to $\text{w}/\text{w}$ percent purity, stability studies, shelf-life modeling.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Positioning a Substance on the Food–Drug Continuum

**Objective:** Given the concentration of a bioactive, its specificity of action, and regulatory class, derive the FNP score.

**Step 1 — Standardize each input on a 0–10 scale.**

$$
C_{\text{norm}} = \frac{C - C_{\min}}{C_{\max} - C_{\min}} \times 10
$$

This converts raw concentration (in $\text{mg}/\text{g}$) into a normalized value.

**Step 2 — Specificity score $S$** is empirically assigned:
- $S = 1$: Broad nutritional support (e.g., Vitamin C in orange).
- $S = 5$: Targeted organ effect (e.g., lutein for retina).
- $S = 10$: Single-receptor pharmacology (e.g., synthetic statin).

**Step 3 — Regulation score $R$** is assigned per national framework:

| Category | $R$ Value |
|---|---|
| Ordinary food | 1 |
| Functional food (FSSAI/FOSHU) | 4 |
| Health supplement | 7 |
| Nutraceutical (regulated dose form) | 8 |
| Pharmaceutical (Rx only) | 10 |

**Step 4 — Apply weights.** For KTU purposes, default weights:

$$
w_1 = 0.3,\quad w_2 = 0.4,\quad w_3 = 0.3
$$

**Step 5 — Compute final FNP score.**

$$
\text{Score}_{\text{FNP}} = (0.3 \cdot C_{\text{norm}}) + (0.4 \cdot S) + (0.3 \cdot R)
$$

**Step 6 — Classify the result.**

$$
\text{Classification} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Ordinary Food}, & 0 \leq \text{Score}_{\text{FNP}} < 3 \\
\text{Functional Food}, & 3 \leq \text{Score}_{\text{FNP}} < 6 \\
\text{Nutraceutical}, & 6 \leq \text{Score}_{\text{FNP}} < 8 \\
\text{Pharmaceutical}, & 8 \leq \text{Score}_{\text{FNP}} \leq 10
\end{cases}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A beverage contains $C = 50\,\text{mg}/\text{L}$ quercetin (assume $C_{\min}=0, C_{\max}=100$), acts on the vascular endothelium ($S = 6$), and is sold as a **FSSAI health supplement** ($R = 7$).

**Step 1:** Normalize concentration.

$$
C_{\text{norm}} = \frac{50 - 0}{100 - 0} \times 10 = 5.0
$$

**Step 2:** Specificity value is given.

$$
S = 6
$$

**Step 3:** Regulation value is given.

$$
R = 7
$$

**Step 4:** Apply weights.

$$
\text{Score}_{\text{FNP}} = (0.3 \times 5.0) + (0.4 \times 6) + (0.3 \times 7)
$$

**Step 5:** Expand each term.

$$
= 1.5 + 2.4 + 2.1 = 6.0
$$

**Step 6:** Classify.

$$
\text{Score}_{\text{FNP}} = 6.0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Nutraceutical (boundary with Functional Food)}
$$

This means the quercetin beverage is a **nutraceutical on the borderline of functional food** — a perfect KTU-style classification answer.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Functional Food vs. Nutraceutical

| Parameter | Functional Food | Nutraceutical |
|---|---|---|
| Form | Conventional food (consumed as-is) | Isolated / concentrated dose form |
| Dose of bioactive | Naturally occurring or fortified | Purified, often higher |
| Claim type | General health / structure-function | Specific disease risk reduction |
| Regulatory burden | Moderate (FSSAI functional food) | Higher (FSSAI nutraceutical / supplement) |
| Examples | Probiotic yogurt, fortified flour | Fish oil capsules, garlic tablets |
| Daily intake | One serving contributes benefit | Capsule, tablet, soft-gel |
| Marketing claim | "Improves digestion" | "Reduces cholesterol" |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Theoretical Insight:** Functional foods are **matrix-bound** (i.e., bioactive is inside the food matrix), while nutraceuticals are **matrix-free** (isolated, extracted, or synthesized). This distinction is the most testable in KTU Part A questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Food–Drug Continuum (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ordinary Food] --> B[Functional Food]
    B --> C[Nutraceutical]
    C --> D[Pharmaceutical]
    A1[Apple, Rice, Milk] -.-> A
    B1[Probiotic Yogurt, Omega-3 Milk] -.-> B
    C1[Fish Oil Capsule, Garlic Extract] -.-> C
    D1[Atorvastatin, Metformin] -.-> D
```

### 4.2 Classification Tree of Nutraceuticals

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    N[NUTRACEUTICALS]
    N --> P[Plant Derived]
    N --> Z[Animal Derived]
    N --> M[Microbial Derived]
    P --> P1[Carotenoids]
    P --> P2[Flavonoids]
    P --> P3[Phytoestrogens]
    P --> P4[Glucosinolates]
    P --> P5[Saponins]
    Z --> Z1[Omega 3 PUFA]
    Z --> Z2[Conjugated Linoleic Acid]
    Z --> Z3[Chondroitin and Glucosamine]
    M --> M1[Probiotics]
    M --> M2[Prebiotics]
    M --> M3[Synbiotics]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Health Benefits Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ING[Bioactive Ingredient] --> BIO[Biological Action]
    BIO --> T1[Antioxidant Defense]
    BIO --> T2[Anti Inflammatory Action]
    BIO --> T3[Immune Modulation]
    BIO --> T4[Cholesterol Reduction]
    T1 --> H1[Reduced Oxidative Stress]
    T2 --> H2[Lower Chronic Inflammation]
    T3 --> H3[Improved Resistance]
    T4 --> H4[Cardiovascular Protection]
    H1 --> OUT[Overall Wellness and Disease Risk Reduction]
    H2 --> OUT
    H3 --> OUT
    H4 --> OUT
```

### 4.4 Regulatory Spectrum (Indian Context)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[FSSAI Ordinary Foods] --> R2[FSSAI Functional Foods]
    R2 --> R3[FSSAI Health Supplements]
    R3 --> R4[FSSAI Nutraceuticals]
    R4 --> R5[CDSCO Pharmaceutical Drugs]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note for KTU:**
> When asked "How is a functional food different from a nutraceutical?", the answer should reference that **functional foods remain in the food matrix** (left side of architecture) while **nutraceuticals are isolated, concentrated doses** (right side), with progressively stricter regulatory oversight.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the term "Nutraceutical." Who coined the term, and in which year?*

**Model Answer:**
The term *Nutraceutical* was coined by **Dr. Stephen DeFelice** in **1989**. It is defined as a substance which is a **food or part of a food** and provides **medical or health benefits**, including the **prevention** and **treatment** of disease. Examples include omega-3 capsules, garlic tablets, and lycopene-enriched tomato extracts.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between a Functional Food and a Nutraceutical with two examples each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Functional Food | Nutraceutical |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Conventional food providing health benefit beyond basic nutrition | Isolated bioactive in concentrated dose form |
| Matrix | Bioactive within food matrix | Bioactive in extracted/dose form |
| Example | Probiotic curd, fortified salt | Fish oil capsule, ginseng tablet |
| Claim strength | General/structure-function | Disease risk-reduction |

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do NOT confuse *FOSHU* (Japan) with *DeFelice* (USA) — examiners frequently test this.
> - Do NOT write "nutraceutical = food + medicine" generically; the official DeFelice definition **must be quoted** to earn full marks.
> - Always mention the **FSSAI 2016 Regulations** when discussing Indian regulatory aspects.
> - For classification questions, give **at least two examples** per category.
> - Spelling errors in "DeFelice" / "Nutraceutical" cost 0.5 marks per board policy.

---

#### ✏️ Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Define functional foods and nutraceuticals. Explain the historical evolution and milestones leading to their formal recognition. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Classify nutraceuticals based on source and chemical nature, providing suitable examples for each category. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

1. **Definitions (2 marks):**
   - *Functional Food:* A conventional or modified food providing health benefits beyond basic nutrition (Japan FOSHU framework, 1991).
   - *Nutraceutical:* A food or food part delivering medical/health benefit, including prevention and treatment of disease (DeFelice, 1989).

2. **Historical Evolution (3 marks):**
   - 1980s Japan — concept of *Kinousei Shokuhin* arises.
   - **1989** — DeFelice coins "Nutraceutical."
   - **1991** — Japan formalizes FOSHU regulatory system.
   - **1994** — USA DSHEA defines dietary supplements.
   - **2002** — EU PASSCLAIM consensus.
   - **2006** — FSSAI Act.
   - **2016** — FSSAI-NF Regulations for nutraceuticals/functional foods in India.

3. **Drivers and Significance (2 marks):**
   - Rising lifestyle diseases (obesity, diabetes, CVD).
   - Increased healthcare cost burden.
   - Consumer shift toward preventive wellness.
   - Technological advances in food processing (microencapsulation, nano-delivery).

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

| Class | Source | Example Compound | Source Food |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carotenoids | Plant | $\beta$-carotene, Lycopene | Carrot, Tomato |
| Flavonoids | Plant | Quercetin, Catechin | Onion, Tea |
| Phytoestrogens | Plant | Genistein | Soybean |
| Glucosinolates | Plant | Sulforaphane | Broccoli |
| Saponins | Plant | Ginsenoside | Ginseng |
| Omega-3 PUFA | Animal | EPA, DHA | Fish oil |
| CLA | Animal | Conjugated Linoleic Acid | Dairy |
| Probiotics | Microbial | *Lactobacillus* | Yogurt |
| Prebiotics | Microbial | FOS, Inulin | Chicory |

A diagrammatic classification (see Section 4.2) supports this answer. Each correct example with source earns credit.

---

#### ✏️ Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative to Question A)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the Food–Drug Continuum model with a neat diagram. Discuss how a substance can be classified on this continuum. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the role of FSSAI in regulating nutraceuticals and functional foods in India. Mention the categories covered under FSSAI-NF Regulations, 2016. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

1. **Concept (2 marks):**
   The Food–Drug Continuum (also called the FNP Spectrum) places every ingested substance along a gradient from *Ordinary Food → Functional Food → Nutraceutical → Pharmaceutical*, based on concentration, specificity, and regulatory class.

2. **Continuum Diagram (2 marks):**
   Refer to Section 4.1 diagram. Food (apple) → Functional Food (probiotic yogurt) → Nutraceutical (garlic tablet) → Pharmaceutical (atorvastatin).

3. **Classification Logic (3 marks):**
   - Ordinary Food — general nutrition.
   - Functional Food — enhanced by bioactive or probiotic content.
   - Nutraceutical — extracted/isolated compound in dose form.
   - Pharmaceutical — synthetic or highly purified drug under prescription.

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

1. **FSSAI's Role (3 marks):**
   Established under the **Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006**, FSSAI is the apex regulatory body. In **2016**, it notified the **Food Safety and Standards (Food or Health Supplements, Nutraceuticals, Foods for Special Dietary Use, Foods for Special Medical Purposes, Functional Foods, and Novel Foods) Regulations, 2016**.

2. **Categories Covered (4 marks):**
   - **Health Supplements**
   - **Nutraceuticals**
   - **Foods for Special Dietary Use (FSDU)**
   - **Foods for Special Medical Purposes (FSMP)**
   - **Functional Foods**
   - **Novel Foods**

3. The regulations mandate **safety assessment, ingredient approval, labeling, claims, and traceability** for market authorization.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Nutraceutical** = **Nutrition + Pharmaceutical**; coined by **Stephen DeFelice in 1989**.
- ✅ **Functional Food** concept originated in **Japan** in the **1980s**; FOSHU regulated in **1991**.
- ✅ **FNP Spectrum:** **F**ood → **F**unctional Food → **N**utraceutical → **P**harmaceutical.
- ✅ Functional foods retain the **food matrix**; nutraceuticals are **isolated concentrates**.
- ✅ Major classifications: **Source-based (Plant / Animal / Microbial)**, **Chemical-based (Carotenoids, Flavonoids, etc.)**, and **Regulatory-based (Established/Accepted/Proposed)**.
- ✅ Indian regulation: **FSSAI Act 2006** + **FSSAI-NF Regulations 2016**.
- ✅ Key terms: Phytochemical, Zoochemical, FFHP, Probiotic, Prebiotic, Synbiotic, Designer Food.
- ✅ Key metrics (less common in exam but bonus marks if quoted): **ORAC, NOAEL, DRV, Bioavailability (F)**.
- ✅ Examples to memorize:
  - **Carotenoids** → carrot, tomato
  - **Flavonoids** → tea, onion
  - **Omega-3** → fish oil
  - **Probiotic** → yogurt
  - **Phytoestrogen** → soy
- ✅ **Health benefits** include antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, immunomodulatory, hypocholesterolemic actions.
- ✅ Remember the DeFelice definition **verbatim** for full marks in KTU Part A.
- ✅ Avoid common confusions: FOSHU ≠ DeFelice; Nutraceutical ≠ Pharmaceutical; Functional Food ≠ Probiotic Food alone.

> 🎓 **Final Exam Tip:**
> In KTU theory papers, always structure answers with: **(1) Definition → (2) Year / Origin → (3) Classification → (4) Examples → (5) Indian regulation (FSSAI 2016)**. This 5-point structure is the most mark-efficient template for any introductory nutraceuticals question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Historical Perspective of Nutraceuticals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT863 - NUTRACEUTICALS AND FUNCTIONAL FOODS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Historical Perspective of Nutraceuticals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Historical Perspective of Nutraceuticals

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nutraceutical Definition (Dr. Stephen DeFelice, 1989):**
> A **nutraceutical** is any substance (or a part of a food) that provides medical or health benefits, including the prevention and treatment of disease. The term is a portmanteau of **"nutrition"** and **"pharmaceutical"** — coined by **Dr. Stephen DeFelice**, founder and chairman of the **Foundation for Innovation in Medicine (FIM)**, Cranford, New Jersey, in **1989**.

In the KTU 2024 framework, nutraceuticals are positioned at the intersection of **food science**, **pharmacology**, and **preventive medicine**. They include:
- Dietary supplements
- Functional foods
- Medicinal foods
- Isolated nutrients
- Herbal products
- Bioactive compounds (e.g., polyphenols, carotenoids, omega-3 fatty acids)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Conceptual Hierarchy:** The KTU 2024 Scheme clearly differentiates the three pillars of modern food therapeutics:
> 1. **Food** — basic nutrition (carbohydrates, proteins, fats, vitamins, minerals) for sustenance.
> 2. **Functional Food** — food with demonstrated physiological benefits beyond basic nutrition (e.g., probiotic yogurt, fortified cereals).
> 3. **Nutraceutical** — a concentrated bioactive component delivered in a non-food matrix (capsule, tablet, powder) for therapeutic/clinical benefit.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Bridge" Analogy:**
> Imagine a long suspension bridge. On one end stands **Food** (basic nutrition for life), and on the other end stands **Pharmaceutical Drug** (targeted disease treatment). Somewhere in the middle of this bridge stands **Nutraceutical** — a product that "crosses over" from the world of food into the world of medicine. It is *not* a drug in the legal sense, but it *acts* like one in preventing or managing disease. Just as a pedestrian halfway across the bridge is neither at the starting city nor the destination city, a nutraceutical is neither pure food nor pure drug — it is the **functional midpoint**.

A simpler real-world analogy: think of **turmeric milk** (golden milk) consumed traditionally in Indian households. The **turmeric** is a food spice, but the **curcumin** within it has documented anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and even anticancer properties. When you extract and concentrate curcumin into a capsule, you have effectively created a **nutraceutical** from a traditional food ingredient.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Origin | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| **Nutraceutical** | *Nutrition* + *Pharmaceutical* | Food or food-derived product with therapeutic benefit |
| **Functional Food** | Japan, 1980s | Modified food with health-promoting properties |
| **Phytochemical** | Greek *phyto* (plant) | Bioactive plant compound |
| **Dietary Supplement** | DSHEA, USA, 1994 | Concentrated nutrient in non-food form |
| **FOSHU** | Japan, 1991 | "Foods for Specified Health Uses" — regulatory category |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Knowledge Sheet

## 2.1 The Historical Trajectory: From "Let Food Be Thy Medicine" to Modern Nutraceuticals

The concept that food influences health is **not new** — it is one of humanity's oldest ideas. What is new is the *scientific validation, isolation, and commercialization* of these principles, which is what the nutraceutical industry represents.

### 2.2 Ancient & Pre-Modern Foundations (Before 1900s)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hippocrates (460–377 BCE), the Father of Medicine, famously declared:**
> *"Let food be thy medicine and medicine be thy food."*
> This single aphorism is widely regarded as the **philosophical foundation** of the entire nutraceutical movement.

- **Egyptian Medicine (~1550 BCE):** The **Ebers Papyrus** (one of the oldest known medical texts) documented the use of garlic, coriander, and juniper for treating ailments — these are among the earliest recorded "functional food" applications.
- **Ayurveda (India, ~600 BCE):** The classical Ayurvedic texts (Charaka Samhita, Sushruta Samhita) classified foods (*Ahara*) as a primary pillar of health (*Swasthya*). Herbs like **Ashwagandha**, **Turmeric (Curcuma longa)**, **Tulsi (Ocimum sanctum)**, and **Amla (Phyllanthus emblica)** were prescribed for both nutrition and therapy.
- **Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM, ~200 BCE):** The *Shennong Ben Cao Jing* (Divine Farmer's Materia Medica) catalogued 365 medicinal substances, mostly botanical, categorized into superior, medium, and inferior herbs.
- **Greek & Roman Medicine:** Dioscorides' *De Materia Medica* (50–70 CE) was the foremost pharmacological text for over 1,500 years.

### 2.3 Modern Foundations (1900s–1980s)

- **Vitamin Discovery Era (1910s–1940s):** Casimir Funk coined the term **"vitamine"** in 1912. Isolation of Vitamin C, thiamine, and other micronutrients transformed food science.
- **1940s–1960s:** Development of **food fortification** programs (iodized salt, vitamin-D fortified milk, folate-enriched flour) — the earliest public-health nutraceutical interventions.
- **1970s:** Japanese researchers begin identifying **"functional food components"** like oligosaccharides, lactic acid bacteria, and dietary fiber.

### 2.4 The Birth of the Nutraceutical Industry (1989–2000s)

- **1989:** Dr. **Stephen DeFelice** coins the term "nutraceutical" — recognized as the official birth year of the industry.
- **1991:** Japan introduces the **FOSHU (Food for Specified Health Uses)** regulatory system — the world's first formal regulatory category for health-claim foods.
- **1994:** The United States passes the **Dietary Supplement Health and Education Act (DSHEA)** — defining dietary supplements legally and separating them from drugs.
- **Late 1990s:** Explosion of **omega-3 fatty acid**, **probiotic**, and **antioxidant** supplements in global markets.

### 2.5 Maturation & Globalization (2000s–Present)

- **2000s:** European Food Safety Authority (**EFSA**) begins regulating health claims under the **Nutrition and Health Claims Regulation (EC) No 1924/2006**.
- **2010s:** India establishes the **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)** in 2006 (operational from 2011), bringing nutraceuticals under the **Food Safety and Standards (Health Supplements, Nutraceuticals, Food for Special Dietary Use, Food for Special Medical Purpose, Functional Food and Novel Food) Regulations, 2016**.
- **2020s:** Global nutraceutical market valued at over **USD 400 billion** with a projected CAGR of ~**8%**. Personalized nutrition, microbiome-targeted bioactives, and plant-based proteins dominate the new wave.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Definition Box:**
> - **Nutraceutical** (DeFelice, 1989) = a food (or part of a food) that provides medical or health benefits, including prevention and treatment of disease.
> - **Functional Food** = a food that is similar in appearance to conventional food, consumed as part of the normal diet, with demonstrated physiological benefit beyond basic nutrition.
> - **Medical Food** = a food formulated for enteral use under medical supervision for patients with specific disease states (e.g., PKU formulas).

### 2.6 KTU 2024 Quick-Recall Comparative Table

| Era | Key Event / Concept | Region | Significance |
|------|-------------------|--------|--------------|
| ~460 BCE | Hippocrates — "Food as medicine" | Greece | Philosophical foundation |
| ~1550 BCE | Ebers Papyrus (garlic, juniper) | Egypt | Earliest documented functional foods |
| ~600 BCE | Ayurveda — Ashwagandha, Turmeric | India | Classical food-as-medicine system |
| 1912 | Casimir Funk — "Vitamine" | Europe/USA | Birth of micronutrient science |
| 1984 | Term "Functional Food" popularized | Japan | Foreshadowing of FOSHU |
| 1989 | Dr. Stephen DeFelice coins "Nutraceutical" | USA | **Birth year of nutraceuticals** |
| 1991 | FOSHU system established | Japan | First regulatory framework |
| 1994 | DSHEA passed | USA | Legal definition of dietary supplements |
| 2006 | EC 1924/2006 Regulation | Europe | EU health-claim framework |
| 2016 | FSSAI Nutraceutical Regulations | India | Indian regulatory framework |

### 2.7 Why "Historical Perspective" Matters in KTU Examinations

The KTU 2024 Scheme Question Paper pattern for **PEFTT863** frequently tests:
1. **Define nutraceutical and give its origin** (3-mark short answer — almost every exam).
2. **Explain the historical evolution** of nutraceuticals with milestones (14-mark long answer).
3. **Compare ancient food-medicine systems with modern nutraceuticals**.
4. **Discuss FOSHU, DSHEA, and FSSAI** in relation to nutraceutical regulation.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** Always mention the **year 1989** and **Dr. Stephen DeFelice** when defining nutraceuticals. Marks are often lost when students forget the name and year of origin.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Evolution Matrix

## 3.1 The Five-Stage Evolution of Food → Nutraceutical

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to articulate a **clear evolution** from generic food to specialized nutraceuticals. The following five stages are the accepted KTU-recommended progression:

$$
\boxed{\text{Stage 1: Food} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Stage 2: Fortified Food} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Stage 3: Functional Food} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Stage 4: Dietary Supplement} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Stage 5: Nutraceutical}}
$$

### Step 1: Stage 1 — Conventional Food (Pre-historic to 1900s)

**Logic:**
- Early humans consumed food primarily for **sustenance and energy**.
- The connection between food and health was **observational** ("eating liver cured night blindness" — which we now know is due to Vitamin A).
- There was **no scientific understanding** of *why* certain foods improved health.

$$
\text{Purpose of Food}_{\text{Stage 1}} = \text{Sustenance} + \text{Basic Energy Provision}
$$

### Step 2: Stage 2 — Fortified Food (1900s–1950s)

**Logic:**
- With the discovery of **vitamins and minerals**, governments and food manufacturers began **adding** micronutrients to staple foods.
- Examples: **iodized salt** (prevention of goiter), **vitamin D-fortified milk** (rickets prevention), **folic acid-fortified flour** (neural tube defect prevention).

$$
\text{Fortified Food} = \text{Conventional Food} + \text{Added Essential Nutrient(s)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Fortified foods are **still** foods — they only restore nutrients lost in processing or prevent widespread deficiency.

### Step 3: Stage 3 — Functional Food (1980s–1990s)

**Logic:**
- The **Japanese FOSHU system (1991)** formalized the idea that certain foods could deliver *health benefits beyond basic nutrition*.
- A functional food is consumed **as part of the normal diet** (e.g., probiotic yogurt, omega-3 enriched eggs, plant sterol margarines).
- It is *not* a pill, capsule, or any pharmaceutical dosage form.

$$
\text{Functional Food} = \text{Conventional Food} + \text{Bioactive Component at Functional Dose}
$$

### Step 4: Stage 4 — Dietary Supplement (1994 — DSHEA, USA)

**Logic:**
- The **Dietary Supplement Health and Education Act (DSHEA) of 1994** legally defined dietary supplements as products intended to **supplement the diet**, containing one or more dietary ingredients (vitamins, minerals, herbs, amino acids, etc.).
- Supplements are sold as **capsules, tablets, softgels, liquids, or powders** — *not* in conventional food form.

$$
\text{Dietary Supplement} = \text{Concentrated Bioactive} + \text{Non-Food Dosage Form}
$$

### Step 5: Stage 5 — Nutraceutical (1989 — Present)

**Logic:**
- A nutraceutical is a **pharmaceutical-grade** bioactive compound, often **isolated, purified, and standardized** from a food source, delivered in a non-food matrix for a **specific therapeutic purpose**.
- The defining feature of a nutraceutical is its **demonstrated clinical efficacy** in preventing, treating, or managing disease.

$$
\text{Nutraceutical} = \text{Isolated Food Bioactive} + \text{Therapeutic Claim} + \text{Clinical Evidence}
$$

### 3.2 The KTU 2024 Comparative Evolution Matrix

| Parameter | Conventional Food | Fortified Food | Functional Food | Dietary Supplement | Nutraceutical |
|-----------|------------------|----------------|-----------------|-------------------|---------------|
| **Primary Purpose** | Nutrition, energy | Prevent deficiency | Promote health | Supplement diet | Prevent/treat disease |
| **Form** | Whole food | Whole food | Whole food | Capsule, tablet | Capsule, powder, liquid |
| **Active Component** | Intrinsic nutrients | Added micronutrient | Added/detected bioactives | Concentrated nutrient | Isolated & standardized bioactive |
| **Claim Type** | None | Nutritional claim | Structure/function claim | Structure/function claim | Health/therapeutic claim |
| **Regulatory Class** | Food | Food | Food (e.g., FOSHU) | DSHEA-regulated | Food supplement / borderline drug |
| **Example** | Rice, milk, eggs | Iodized salt, D-milk | Probiotic yogurt, omega-3 eggs | Multivitamin tablet | Curcumin capsule, CoQ10, lutein |
| **Stage in Evolution** | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | **5 (Final)** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Trick Question Alert:** Examiners may ask *"Is a multivitamin a nutraceutical or a dietary supplement?"*
> **Answer:** A daily multivitamin is **primarily a dietary supplement** (Stage 4). It becomes a **nutraceutical** (Stage 5) only if it carries a *therapeutic claim* and uses clinically validated doses of standardized bioactives.

### 3.3 Region-Wise Historical Contributions — KTU Perspective

#### 3.3.1 Indian (Ayurvedic) Perspective
- Classical texts: **Charaka Samhita** (internal medicine), **Sushruta Samhita** (surgery and food therapy).
- **Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera)** — modern research validates adaptogenic, anti-stress, and immunomodulatory properties → candidate nutraceutical.
- **Turmeric (Curcuma longa)** — **curcumin** is now a globally marketed nutraceutical with anti-inflammatory, anticancer, and neuroprotective properties.
- **Amla (Phyllanthus emblica)** — one of the richest natural sources of Vitamin C; modern nutraceutical applications in antioxidant and hepatoprotective formulations.

#### 3.3.2 Chinese (TCM) Perspective
- **Ginseng (Panax ginseng)** — ginsenosides studied for cognitive and adaptogenic effects.
- **Green tea (Camellia sinensis)** — **EGCG (epigallocatechin gallate)** a major nutraceutical polyphenol.
- **Lingzhi mushroom (Ganoderma lucidum)** — immunomodulatory nutraceutical.

#### 3.3.3 Western / Modern Perspective
- **Stephen DeFelice (1989):** coined the term.
- **DSHEA (1994):** created the legal category.
- **Functional Food Center (Dallas, Texas):** Dr. Danik Martirosyan's contributions to academic codification.
- **FOSHU (1991):** Japanese regulatory pioneering.

### 3.4 Key Take-Home Equations (Conceptual)

$$
\text{Health Benefit} = f(\text{Bioactive Concentration}, \text{Bioavailability}, \text{Dose}, \text{Duration})
$$

$$
\text{Nutraceutical Development Pathway} = \text{Discovery} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Isolation} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Standardization} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Pre-clinical} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Clinical} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Regulatory} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Market}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A substance is **NOT** a nutraceutical merely because it comes from food. It must traverse the rigorous development pathway above to earn nutraceutical status.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define nutraceuticals. Who coined the term and when? `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **nutraceutical** is a substance or a part of a food that provides medical or health benefits, including the prevention and treatment of disease. The term is a **portmanteau of "nutrition" and "pharmaceutical"**, coined by **Dr. Stephen DeFelice**, founder of the Foundation for Innovation in Medicine (FIM), USA, in the year **1989**. Nutraceuticals include dietary supplements, functional foods, and isolated bioactive compounds such as curcumin, omega-3 fatty acids, probiotics, and lycopene.

---

### Question 2: What is FOSHU? Why is it significant in the history of nutraceuticals? `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **FOSHU** stands for **"Foods for Specified Health Uses"**, a regulatory category introduced by the **Japanese Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare in 1991**. Its significance in nutraceutical history is twofold: **(i)** it was the **world's first formal regulatory system** to recognize and approve health claims for functional foods, and **(ii)** it provided a legal and scientific framework that encouraged the development of functional food research in Japan, which later inspired similar regulatory frameworks worldwide, including the **DSHEA (USA, 1994)** and **FSSAI Nutraceutical Regulations (India, 2016)**.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** write "nutraceutical was coined by DeFelice" without the **year 1989** — marks are deducted for incomplete attribution.
> - **Do NOT** confuse **FOSHU (Japan, 1991)** with **DSHEA (USA, 1994)** — these are *different* countries and *different* regulations.
> - **Do NOT** list nutraceutical examples without classifying them properly (e.g., curcumin = nutraceutical; yogurt with live cultures = functional food).
> - **Do NOT** present ancient food-medicine history as "irrelevant" — examiners *expect* a chronological narrative from Ayurveda/TCM/Egypt → modern nutraceuticals.
> - **Always** conclude with the current market value or recent regulatory update to demonstrate updated knowledge.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): With a neat schematic, explain the historical evolution of nutraceuticals. Discuss the role of FOSHU and DSHEA in shaping the modern nutraceutical industry. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]`

#### Part (a) — Historical Evolution of Nutraceuticals (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

The historical evolution of nutraceuticals can be traced through **five distinct eras**:

**1. Ancient Era (460 BCE – 1800s CE):** The notion that food and health are intertwined dates back to antiquity.
- **Hippocrates (460–377 BCE)** declared *"Let food be thy medicine and medicine be thy food."*
- The **Ebers Papyrus (~1550 BCE, Egypt)** documented medicinal use of garlic, juniper, and coriander.
- **Ayurveda (India, ~600 BCE)** in texts such as the Charaka Samhita prescribed herbs like Ashwagandha, Turmeric, and Amla for both nutrition and therapy.
- **Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM, ~200 BCE)** in the Shennong Ben Cao Jing catalogued over 365 plant-based medicinal substances.
- **Greek/Roman medicine** (Dioscorides' *De Materia Medica*, 50–70 CE) systematized botanical pharmacology.

**2. Discovery of Micronutrients (1900s–1940s):**
- **Casimir Funk (1912)** coined the term "vitamine," launching the modern era of micronutrient science.
- Isolation of **Vitamin C, thiamine, niacin, and Vitamin D** enabled targeted nutritional interventions.

**3. Food Fortification Era (1940s–1970s):**
- Governments implemented **mass fortification** programs: **iodized salt** (Iodine Deficiency Disorders), **vitamin D milk** (Rickets), **folic acid flour** (Neural Tube Defects).
- These were the **first public-health nutraceutical interventions**, although they were not called "nutraceuticals" at the time.

**4. Birth of Nutraceutical Industry (1980s–1990s):**
- **1984:** The term "functional food" was popularized in Japan.
- **1989:** **Dr. Stephen DeFelice** coined the term **"nutraceutical"** — officially recognized as the birth year of the industry.
- **1991:** Japan launched **FOSHU** — the world's first regulatory category for health-claim foods.
- **1994:** USA passed the **DSHEA** (Dietary Supplement Health and Education Act), legally defining dietary supplements.

**5. Modern Global Industry (2000s–Present):**
- **2006:** European Union implemented **EC Regulation 1924/2006** for health claims, administered by **EFSA**.
- **2016:** India established the **FSSAI Nutraceutical Regulations**.
- Global nutraceutical market now valued at **>USD 400 billion**, with personalized nutrition, microbiome-targeted bioactives, and plant-based proteins driving growth.

#### Part (b) — Role of FOSHU and DSHEA (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**FOSHU (Foods for Specified Health Uses, Japan, 1991):**
- FOSHU was the **first formal regulatory framework** in the world to recognize and approve health claims for food products.
- It was established by the **Japanese Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare** in response to growing consumer demand for foods with demonstrated health benefits and the rapidly aging Japanese population.
- To qualify for FOSHU approval, a product must have **scientifically proven health benefits**, and its efficacy must be verified by clinical evidence.
- FOSHU products must display the **FOSHU seal** on packaging, giving consumers a clear mark of regulatory approval.
- FOSHU **shaped the modern nutraceutical industry** by:
  - Setting a **gold standard** for health-claim substantiation.
  - Encouraging **R&D investment** in functional food science.
  - Inspiring similar regulatory frameworks in the **USA, EU, India, and other Asian countries**.

**DSHEA (Dietary Supplement Health and Education Act, USA, 1994):**
- DSHEA was a landmark US federal law that **legally defined and regulated dietary supplements** as a distinct category of food.
- Under DSHEA, dietary supplements are defined as products containing one or more dietary ingredients (vitamins, minerals, herbs, amino acids, etc.) intended to **supplement the diet**.
- DSHEA **shaped the modern nutraceutical industry** by:
  - Allowing manufacturers to make **structure/function claims** (e.g., "supports immune health") without FDA pre-approval.
  - Shifting the regulatory burden — the FDA must prove a product is *unsafe* rather than the manufacturer proving it is *safe*.
  - Creating a **massive commercial market** in the USA, which became the world's largest nutraceutical market.

**Comparative Impact:**

| Aspect | FOSHU (Japan, 1991) | DSHEA (USA, 1994) |
|--------|---------------------|-------------------|
| **Scope** | Functional foods | Dietary supplements |
| **Pre-market approval** | Required | Not required |
| **Type of claim** | Specific health claims | Structure/function claims |
| **Pioneering contribution** | First health-claim regulation | First dietary supplement law |

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** FOSHU and DSHEA, established in the early 1990s, are widely regarded as the **two foundational regulatory pillars** of the modern global nutraceutical industry. FOSHU emphasized **scientific validation of health claims**, while DSHEA emphasized **market freedom and consumer access** — together, they created the dual-engine of innovation and growth that defines the nutraceutical sector today.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Differentiate between food, functional food, and nutraceutical. Discuss the contribution of Ayurveda and Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) to the historical development of nutraceuticals. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`

#### Part (a) — Differentiation between Food, Functional Food, and Nutraceutical (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Food | Functional Food | Nutraceutical |
|-----------|------|-----------------|---------------|
| **Definition** | Substance consumed for basic nutrition and energy | Food with demonstrated health benefits beyond basic nutrition | Isolated bioactive from food with therapeutic/clinical efficacy |
| **Form** | Whole, natural | Whole food (modified or enriched) | Capsule, tablet, powder, liquid |
| **Primary Purpose** | Sustain life and provide energy | Promote health and reduce disease risk | Prevent, manage, or treat disease |
| **Examples** | Rice, wheat, milk, eggs | Probiotic yogurt, omega-3 eggs, fortified cereals, plant-sterol margarine | Curcumin capsules, CoQ10 tablets, glucosamine, lycopene |
| **Regulatory Class** | Food | Functional food (FOSHU, EFSA, FSSAI) | Food supplement / borderline drug |
| **Historical Stage** | Stage 1 | Stage 3 | Stage 5 |
| **Claim Type** | None | Structure/function claim | Health/therapeutic claim |
| **Mode of Consumption** | Part of regular diet | Part of regular diet | As supplement (not whole food) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** The **dosage form** is the clearest differentiator — if it looks and is consumed like food (yogurt, bread, milk), it is a *functional food*. If it is in a pharmaceutical-like form (capsule, tablet, powder), it is a *nutraceutical*.

#### Part (b) — Contribution of Ayurveda and TCM (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Ayurveda (India, ~600 BCE):**
- The word "Ayurveda" literally means **"the science of life"** (*Ayur* = life, *Veda* = knowledge).
- Classical texts: **Charaka Samhita** (internal medicine and nutrition), **Sushruta Samhita** (surgery and food therapy), and **Ashtanga Hridaya**.
- Ayurveda classifies food (*Ahara*) as one of the **three pillars of health** (*Traya Upastambha*) along with sleep and celibacy.
- Key Ayurvedic herbs that have become modern nutraceuticals:
  - **Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera):** Adaptogenic, anti-stress, immunomodulatory; commercial nutraceutical in the global market.
  - **Turmeric (Curcuma longa):** Source of **curcumin**, a powerful anti-inflammatory and antioxidant nutraceutical.
  - **Amla (Phyllanthus emblica):** One of the richest natural sources of Vitamin C; modern nutraceutical applications in antioxidant and hepatoprotective formulations.
  - **Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri):** Nootropic, used in cognitive-health nutraceuticals.
  - **Triphala:** A polyherbal formulation widely studied for gut and metabolic health.
- The FSSAI 2016 regulations in India formally recognize the **Ayurvedic heritage** as a foundation for modern nutraceutical development.

**Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM, ~200 BCE):**
- The classical text **Shennong Ben Cao Jing** (*Divine Farmer's Materia Medica*) catalogued 365 medicinal substances, categorized into superior (non-toxic, for long-term use), medium, and inferior herbs.
- The text **Huangdi Neijing** (*Yellow Emperor's Classic of Internal Medicine*) established the theoretical foundations of TCM, including the concept of *yin-yang* balance achieved through food.
- Key TCM substances that have become modern nutraceuticals:
  - **Ginseng (Panax ginseng):** Active compounds **ginsenosides** are studied for adaptogenic, cognitive, and anti-fatigue properties.
  - **Green tea (Camellia sinensis):** Source of **EGCG (epigallocatechin gallate)**, a major polyphenol nutraceutical with antioxidant and anticancer properties.
  - **Lingzhi/Reishi mushroom (Ganoderma lucidum):** Triterpenes and polysaccharides studied for immunomodulatory and hepatoprotective effects.
  - **Goji berry (Lycium barbarum):** Rich in carotenoids and polysaccharides; modern nutraceutical for eye health and immunity.
  - **Astragalus (Astragalus membranaceus):** Immunomodulatory nutraceutical with growing global demand.

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** The historical knowledge of Ayurveda and TCM constitutes the **empirical foundation** of the modern nutraceutical industry. Many of today's blockbuster nutraceuticals — curcumin, ashwagandha, ginseng, EGCG — were used therapeutically for centuries before modern science validated their bioactive compounds and mechanisms. The nutraceutical industry is, in essence, a **scientific continuation** of these ancient food-as-medicine traditions.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Historical Perspective of Nutraceuticals**

- **Definition:** A nutraceutical is a food or part of a food that provides medical or health benefits, including prevention and treatment of disease.
- **Term Origin:** Coined by **Dr. Stephen DeFelice** in **1989**, USA; portmanteau of **"nutrition"** + **"pharmaceutical"**.
- **Foundation:** Hippocrates' aphorism *"Let food be thy medicine"* (460 BCE).
- **Ancient Systems:** **Ayurveda (India)**, **Traditional Chinese Medicine (China)**, **Ebers Papyrus (Egypt)**, **Greek/Roman medicine**.
- **Modern Milestones:** **Casimir Funk's "vitamine" (1912)** → **food fortification (1940s)** → **functional food concept (1980s)** → **DeFelice's "nutraceutical" (1989)** → **FOSHU Japan (1991)** → **DSHEA USA (1994)** → **EFSA EU (2006)** → **FSSAI India (2016)**.
- **FOSHU (1991):** World's first formal regulatory system for health-claim foods; Japan.
- **DSHEA (1994):** Legal definition of dietary supplements in the USA; allows structure/function claims.
- **Five-Stage Evolution:** **Food → Fortified Food → Functional Food → Dietary Supplement → Nutraceutical**.
- **Indian Nutraceuticals:** Curcumin, Ashwagandha, Amla, Brahmi, Triphala.
- **Chinese Nutraceuticals:** Ginseng (ginsenosides), Green tea (EGCG), Lingzhi/Reishi, Goji berry, Astragalus.
- **Current Market:** Global nutraceutical market valued at **>USD 400 billion**; CAGR ~**8%**.
- **Key Distinction:** Functional food = food form; Nutraceutical = pharmaceutical-like form with therapeutic claims.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls to Avoid in KTU Exams:**
> - Confusing **FOSHU (1991, Japan)** with **DSHEA (1994, USA)** — different countries, different regulations, different years.
> - Writing "DeFelice coined the term" without **1989** and without mentioning **FIM (Foundation for Innovation in Medicine)**.
> - Confusing dietary supplement (Stage 4) with nutraceutical (Stage 5) — supplements are *not* automatically nutraceuticals.
> - Ignoring ancient traditions (Ayurveda, TCM) — examiners expect chronological coverage.
> - Omitting examples — always cite at least 2–3 specific nutraceuticals (curcumin, omega-3, EGCG, etc.) in long answers.
