topics.title: EU Guidelines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT863 - NUTRACEUTICALS AND FUNCTIONAL FOODS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** EU Guidelines # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **EU Guidelines** governing nutraceuticals and functional foods constitute a harmonised, science-based regulatory framework administered primarily by the **European Commission (EC)** and evaluated by the **European Food Safety Authority (EFSA)** headquartered in **Parma, Italy**. The framework ensures that any product marketed within the European Union (EU) bearing nutritional, health, or disease-risk reduction claims is substantiated by **generally accepted scientific evidence (GASE)** and authorised through a centralised procedure before consumer exposure. The five pillars (core regulations) of the EU framework are: 1. **Regulation (EC) No 1924/2006** — Nutrition & Health Claims Regulation (NHCR) 2. **Regulation (EU) 2015/2283** — Novel Food Regulation (repealed 258/97) 3. **Directive 2002/46/EC** — Food Supplements Directive 4. **Regulation (EU) No 609/2013** — Foods for Specific Groups (repealed PARNUTS Directive 89/398/EEC) 5. **Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008** — Food Additives Regulation > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For board examinations, students must remember that the EU operates a **pre-market authorisation system** for novel foods and a **pre-approval system for health claims**, distinguishing it sharply from the **post-market surveillance model** followed in the USA under DSHEA 1994. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The EU as a Strict School Principal" Imagine the EU market as a **highly disciplined international school** where every student (food product) must pass a **rigorous admission interview** (scientific assessment by EFSA) before being allowed inside the playground (EU consumer market). The school principal (European Commission) maintains a strict **"Health Claims Register"** — a published list of every claim allowed. Any product that "whispers" unverified benefits on its label is expelled. The EU's philosophy: **"Prove it before you sell it."** In contrast, the **US system** is more like a permissive open campus where students can enroll freely but are monitored afterwards. This is why nutraceutical companies often **launch first in the USA** and **apply for EU approval later**. ## 1.3 The Three Pillars of EU Regulatory Logic | Pillar | Principle | Practical Consequence | |--------|-----------|----------------------| | **Safety First** | Substantial equivalence + toxicological dossier | Every novel ingredient must undergo 90-day sub-chronic toxicity studies | | **Scientific Substantiation** | EFSA peer-review by independent scientists | No proprietary "trade secret" exemptions | | **Consumer Protection** | Mandatory labelling & nutrient profiles | Fortification thresholds capped at **15 % of NRV** per 100 g/ml | > [!NOTE] > **Key Board Term — "Nutrient Reference Value (NRV)"** in the EU is set at the daily intake recommended for an average adult (≈ 8 400 kJ / 2 000 kcal diet), e.g., Vitamin C NRV = **80 mg/day**, Calcium NRV = **800 mg/day**. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Regulation (EC) 1924/2006 — The Nutrition & Health Claims Regulation (NHCR) This is the **cornerstone regulation** for nutraceutical labelling. It classifies claims into three legally-binding categories: ### 2.1.1 Type of Claims Permitted | Claim Type | Definition | EFSA Example | Approval Status | |------------|------------|--------------|-----------------| | **Nutrition Claim** | States/implies a food has beneficial nutritional properties | "Low fat", "High fibre", "Source of Omega-3" | Pre-defined list in Annex | | **Health Claim** | States/implies a relationship between food/constituent and health | "Calcium helps build strong bones" | EFSA-approved Register | | **Health Claim based on Newly Developed Science** | Proprietary claim with novel data | Botanicals with new evidence | 5-year data protection | ### 2.1.2 Prohibited Health Claims (Article 10) A claim is **automatically rejected** if it: - Claims **disease prevention, treatment, or cure** (e.g., "cures cancer") - States a **rate or amount of weight loss** ("lose 10 kg in 5 days") - Implies that **not consuming the food may harm health** - References **recommendations of individual doctors or health professionals** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Article 14 Disease Risk Reduction Claims (DRRCs)** are permitted ONLY if the claim is scientifically proven and accompanied by a statement that the beneficial effect is obtained by consuming **at least X grams/day** of the food constituent. ## 2.2 Regulation (EU) 2015/2283 — The Novel Food Regulation Effective **1 January 2018**, this replaced the older 1997 framework. A "Novel Food" is defined as any food not used for human consumption to a significant degree in the EU before **15 May 1997**. ### 2.2.1 Categories of Novel Food (Article 3) 1. Food with new molecular structure (engineered nanomaterial) 2. Food consisting of/isolated from microorganisms, fungi, or algae 3. Food consisting of/isolated from plants and their parts (e.g., **Moringa oleifera**) 4. Food consisting of/isolated from animals or their parts 5. Food consisting of engineered nanomaterials 6. Vitamins, minerals, or other substances with altered bioavailability 7. Food obtained through new production processes (e.g., **UV-treated mushrooms** for Vitamin D2) ### 2.2.2 The Authorisation Procedure (Step-by-Step) **Step 1:** Applicant submits dossier to **European Commission** via the e-Submission system. **Step 2:** Commission requests EFSA scientific opinion (timeline: **9 months**, extendable by additional 9 months). **Step 3:** EFSA publishes opinion → **Risk Assessment** stage. **Step 4:** Risk Management → Standing Committee on Plants, Animals, Food and Feed (SCoPAFF) votes. **Step 5:** Authorised foods are added to the **Union List** (Annex I of the Regulation). **Step 6:** Standard 5-year **data protection** for proprietary dossiers. ## 2.3 Directive 2002/46/EC — Food Supplements Directive Harmonises the marketing of vitamin/mineral supplements across EU. Sets: - **Positive lists** of permitted vitamins (A, B-complex, C, D, E, K) and minerals (Ca, Mg, Fe, Zn, Se, etc.) - **Minimum and maximum levels** of vitamins/minerals per daily dose - **Labelling requirements**: name of categories, recommended daily intake, warning "**Food supplements should not be used as a substitute for a varied diet**" > [!NOTE] > Substances other than vitamins/minerals (e.g., botanicals, amino acids, fatty acids) are **NOT regulated** by this Directive. They fall under national legislation until 2020/2024 amendments. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Statutory Limits & Numerical Triggers Since nutraceutical regulation is **quantitative**, several numerical thresholds drive compliance. These are the **must-memorise values** for the KTU board exam. | Parameter | Statutory Value | Regulatory Basis | |-----------|-----------------|------------------| | Daily intake threshold for "high" claim | **≥ 30 % of NRV per 100 g** | NHCR Annex | | Daily intake threshold for "source" claim | **≥ 15 % of NRV per 100 g** | NHCR Annex | | Significant degree of human consumption (Novel Food cut-off) | **15 May 1997** | Reg. 2015/2283 | | EFSA risk assessment default window | **9 months** (max 18) | Reg. 2015/2283 Art. 11 | | Data protection period for novel food dossier | **5 years** | Reg. 2015/2283 Art. 26 | | Minimum vitamin content per daily dose | **15 % of NRV** | Dir. 2002/46/EC | | Maximum safe Vitamin A (retinol) for adults | **800 µg RE/day** | EFSA Tolerable Upper Intake | | Maximum safe Vitamin D for adults | **100 µg/day** (Tolerable Upper Level) | EFSA 2012 opinion | | Maximum safe Vitamin E (α-tocopherol) | **300 mg/day** | EFSA 2006 opinion | ### 2.4.1 Working Numerical Method — Claim Verification To check whether a product qualifies for a **"Source of Vitamin C"** claim, apply: $$ \text{Claim Qualification Ratio} = \frac{\text{Nutrient Content per 100 g (or 100 ml)}}{\text{Nutrient Reference Value (NRV)}} \times 100 \% $$ If the ratio $\geq 15\%$, the product may bear the **"source"** claim. If $\geq 30\%$, it may bear the **"high"** claim. **Real-world utility:** Used by **EU labelling officers** in food industries to design compliant packaging. Failure to meet the 15 % threshold can result in **withdrawal from market** and **penalties up to € 50 000** in member-state jurisdictions. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Numerical Case — Compliance Check for a Functional Yogurt **Problem Statement (KTU Numerical Practice):** A Greek company markets a fruit yogurt containing **Vitamin C = 18 mg per 100 g serving** and **Calcium = 220 mg per 100 g**. Determine whether the product qualifies for the "Source of" claim and the "High" claim for each nutrient. Given: NRV (Vitamin C) = 80 mg; NRV (Calcium) = 800 mg. ### Step 1 — Identify the relevant statutory rule Under NHCR Annex, the qualifying thresholds are **15 % (source)** and **30 % (high)** of NRV per 100 g for solid foods. ### Step 2 — Calculate the Claim Qualification Ratio (CQR) for Vitamin C $$ \text{CQR}_{\text{Vit C}} = \frac{18 \text{ mg}}{80 \text{ mg}} \times 100 $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CQR}_{\text{Vit C}} &= \frac{18}{80} \times 100 \\ &= 0.225 \times 100 \\ &= 22.5 \% \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** 22.5 % ≥ 15 % → **"Source of Vitamin C"** claim is PERMITTED. 22.5 % < 30 % → **"High in Vitamin C"** claim is NOT permitted. ### Step 3 — Calculate the Claim Qualification Ratio (CQR) for Calcium $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CQR}_{\text{Ca}} &= \frac{220 \text{ mg}}{800 \text{ mg}} \times 100 \\ &= 0.275 \times 100 \\ &= 27.5 \% \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** 27.5 % ≥ 15 % → **"Source of Calcium"** claim is PERMITTED. 27.5 % < 30 % → **"High in Calcium"** claim is NOT permitted. ### Step 4 — Final Compliance Summary | Nutrient | Content per 100 g | NRV | CQR (%) | "Source of" (≥15 %) | "High in" (≥30 %) | |----------|-------------------|-----|---------|---------------------|-------------------| | Vitamin C | 18 mg | 80 mg | 22.5 | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | | Calcium | 220 mg | 800 mg | 27.5 | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Practical Takeaway:** To upgrade the product to a "High in Calcium" claim, the manufacturer must either (a) increase Ca content to ≥ 240 mg/100 g, or (b) reduce serving size on the label to 75 g (which would re-anchor the claim per single-serving rules of NHCR). ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Three Core EU Regulations | Analytical Dimension | NHCR 1924/2006 | Novel Food 2015/2283 | Supplements Dir. 2002/46/EC | |----------------------|----------------|----------------------|------------------------------| | **Scope** | All food labelling with nutrition/health claims | Foods not consumed in EU pre-1997 | Vitamin & mineral supplements | | **Trigger Event** | Making a claim | Novel ingredient introduction | Sale as supplement dosage form | | **Authority** | EFSA → Commission | EFSA → Commission | Member State authorities | | **Timeline** | 5 months typical | 9–18 months | Variable | | **Data Protection** | 5 years (Art. 21) | 5 years (Art. 26) | None | | **Pre-Market Approval?** | YES | YES | NO (notification only) | | **Mandatory Labelling?** | Yes | Yes | Yes (Dir. Art. 6) | | **Botanicals Covered?** | Yes (claims only) | Yes (if novel) | NO (national law) | ## 3.3 Worked Case — Novel Food Authorisation Pathway (Moringa Leaf Powder) **Scenario:** A Sri Lankan exporter wishes to sell dried *Moringa oleifera* leaf powder in Germany in 2025 as a food supplement. ### Step 1 — Establish Legal Status The EU Novel Food Catalogue (maintained by EC) lists Moringa leaves as **"not authorised"** for use as food in the EU pre-1997. ### Step 2 — Determine Authorisation Requirement Therefore, **Regulation (EU) 2015/2283** applies. Moringa must undergo full novel food authorisation before placement on the EU market. ### Step 3 — Compile Application Dossier The dossier (per EFSA guidance, 2016) must include: - **(a)** Administrative data (applicant, contact, Member State) - **(b)** Identity and characterisation (taxonomic confirmation, cultivation) - **(c)** Production process details (drying, milling, microbiological quality) - **(d)** Compositional data (macros, micros, anti-nutrients — e.g., glucosinolates) - **(e)** Specifications (heavy metals, pesticides, mycotoxins limits) - **(f)** Proposed uses and use levels - **(g)** Absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion (ADME) data - **(h)** Nutritional information (impact on dietary intake) - **(i)** Toxicological information (90-day sub-chronic rodent study, in vitro genotoxicity) - **(j)** Allergenicity assessment ### Step 4 — Calculate EFSA Timeline $$ \text{Total Statutory Time} = \text{9 (initial)} + \text{9 (extension if requested)} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\min} &= 9 \text{ months} \\ T_{\max} &= 9 + 9 = 18 \text{ months} \end{aligned} $$ The applicant must budget a minimum of **12–24 months** realistic timeline including Commission risk management. ### Step 5 — Cost-Benefit Justification | Parameter | Estimated Value | |-----------|-----------------| | EFSA dossier preparation cost | € 250 000 – € 500 000 | | Toxicology study (90-day rodent) | € 200 000 – € 400 000 | | Total upfront investment | **€ 500 000 – € 1 000 000** | | EU market access revenue (Moringa powder 5 t/year @ € 50/kg) | € 250 000/year | | Payback period | **2–4 years** | This is why most SMEs partner with **EU importers/aggregators** who already hold Moringa novel food approval. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram A — EU Novel Food Authorisation Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Applicant / Industry Consortium] --> B[Dossier Compilation per EFSA Guidance] B --> C[Submission via EC e-Submission Portal] C --> D{Novel Food Status Confirmed?} D -->|Yes| E[EFSA Scientific Risk Assessment 9 to 18 months] D -->|No| Z[Returned to National Law] E --> F[EFSA Scientific Opinion Published] F --> G{Positive Opinion?} G -->|Yes| H[SCoPAFF Risk Management Vote] G -->|No| I[Authorisation Rejected Applicant Notified] H --> J[European Parliament Scrutiny 2 months] J --> K[Implementing Regulation Adopted] K --> L[Entry Added to Union List Annex I] L --> M[5 Year Data Protection Granted] M --> N[Free Placement on EU Market] I --> O[Applicant May Refile with New Data] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Flow:** This is the **linear regulatory pathway**. Notice the three decision gates (rhombuses) D, G, and J — these are the **failure points** where applications are most often rejected. ## 4.2 Diagram B — Claim Classification Architecture under NHCR 1924/2006 ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Food Labelling Communication] --> Q1{Does it state nutritional property?} Q1 -->|Yes| NClaim[Nutrition Claim
Annex Permitted List] Q1 -->|No| Q2{Does it imply health benefit?} Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Does it reference disease risk?} Q3 -->|Yes| DRRC[Article 14 Disease Risk Reduction Claim
EFSA Approval Required] Q3 -->|No| Q4{Based on newly developed science?} Q4 -->|Yes| NDSC[Newly Developed Science Claim
5 Year Data Protection] Q4 -->|No| GHC[General Health Claim
EFSA Register Approved] Q2 -->|No| Generic[Generic Marketing Statement
No EFSA Approval Needed] NClaim --> Stop[Authorised Labelling] DRRC --> Stop NDSC --> Stop GHC --> Stop Generic --> Stop ``` ## 4.3 Diagram C — Block-Level Functional Architecture of EU Food Safety Governance ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph EU_INSTITUTIONS[EU INSTITUTIONS] EC[European Commission
DG SANTE Risk Manager] EP[European Parliament
Co Legislator] COUNCIL[Council of the EU
Member States] end subgraph SCIENTIFIC_BODY[SCIENTIFIC BODY] EFSA[European Food Safety Authority
EFSA Parma
Risk Assessor] SC[EFSA Scientific Panels
Nutrition Dietetics Foods
NDA Panel] end subgraph MEMBER_STATES[MEMBER STATE AUTHORITIES] MS1[National Food Safety Authority
e.g. FSSAI for India BVL for Germany] MS2[Enforcement Officers] end subgraph INDUSTRY[INDUSTRY STAKEHOLDERS] IND[Food and Nutraceutical Manufacturers] ASSOC[Industry Associations ENSA EHPM] end EC -->|Requests Risk Assessment| EFSA EP -->|Adopts Regulations| EC COUNCIL -->|Adopts Regulations| EC EFSA --> SC SC -->|Scientific Opinion| EFSA EFSA -->|Risk Assessment Output| EC EC -->|Implementing Acts| MS1 MS1 -->|Market Surveillance| MS2 MS2 -->|Inspections and Sampling| IND IND -->|Submits Dossiers and Notifications| EC ASSOC -->|Consultation and Lobbying| EC ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Architectural Insight:** EFSA does **NOT** authorise products — it only provides the **scientific opinion**. Authorisation is a **risk management** function of the Commission. This separation of "assessment" from "management" is a cornerstone of the EU food law philosophy enshrined in **Regulation (EC) 178/2002** — the General Food Law Regulation. ## 4.4 Diagram D — Compliance Decision Flow for a Nutraceutical Product Launch ```mermaid flowchart TD A[New Product Concept] --> B{Is it a Food or a Medicine?} B -->|Medicine| MED[Route via EMA Medicinal Products] B -->|Food| F1{Contains Novel Ingredient?} F1 -->|Yes| F2[Novel Food Authorisation 2015/2283] F1 -->|No| F3{Making Health Claim?} F3 -->|Yes| F4[Check EFSA Register of Claims] F3 -->|No| F5[Standard Food Law Applies] F4 -->|Approved Claim| F6[Permitted] F4 -->|Not Approved| F7[Claim Rejected Modify Wording] F2 -->|Authorised| F8[Union List Entry] F2 -->|Not Authorised| F9[Market Blocked in EU] F5 --> END[Launch Permitted] F6 --> END F8 --> END F7 --> A F9 --> REFORM[Reformulate or Apply] REFORM --> A ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]** *"What is meant by a 'Novel Food' under EU Regulation 2015/2283? List any three categories of novel foods."* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Novel Food** is defined under **Article 3 of Regulation (EU) 2015/2283** as any food not used for human consumption to a significant degree in the European Union before **15 May 1997**, irrespective of whether it was used in third countries before that date. Three categories of novel foods include: 1. **Food consisting of or isolated from plants or their parts** — e.g., *Moringa oleifera* leaves, chia seeds. 2. **Engineered nanomaterials** — food with intentionally produced nano-sized particles (< 100 nm). 3. **Food obtained through new production processes** — e.g., UV-treated mushrooms for enhanced Vitamin D2 content, high-pressure preservation. *(1 mark for definition + 0.5 × 3 = 1.5 marks for categories + 0.5 for referencing the 15 May 1997 cut-off date)* ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]** *"Differentiate between a 'nutrition claim' and a 'health claim' under EU Regulation 1924/2006."* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Nutrition Claim | Health Claim | |---------|-----------------|--------------| | **Definition** | States that a food has beneficial nutritional properties (e.g., "low fat", "high in fibre") | States, suggests, or implies a relationship between a food/constituent and health | | **Authorisation** | Pre-defined positive list in Annex | EFSA scientific assessment and inclusion in EU Register | | **Example** | "Source of omega-3 fatty acids" | "Plant sterols lower blood cholesterol" | | **Risk Reduction** | Not permitted | Permitted under Article 14 if substantiated | *(1.5 marks per column differentiation)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Explain the **aims, scope, and key provisions of the EU Novel Food Regulation 2015/2283**. Discuss how it differs from the US Dietary Supplement Health and Education Act (DSHEA) 1994 in terms of pre-market authorisation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **step-by-step authorisation procedure** for placing a novel food ingredient in the EU market. Include a flowchart of the procedure in your answer. **(7 marks)** #### Model Answer — Part (a) **Aims of Regulation (EU) 2015/2283 (2 marks):** - Provide a **single, transparent authorisation procedure** for novel foods across all 27 EU member states. - **Protect human health** by ensuring all novel foods undergo a rigorous EFSA safety assessment before market entry. - **Stimulate innovation** by clarifying the legal pathway for food business operators (FBOs). - Promote **fair trade** while maintaining a high level of consumer protection. **Scope (2 marks):** The regulation applies to all foods intended for human consumption that were not used to a significant degree in the EU before 15 May 1997, including newly developed foods, foods from third-country sources, and foods produced using new technologies. **Key Provisions (1 mark):** Union List (Annex I), centralised authorisation, 5-year data protection, transparency of dossiers. **Comparison with US DSHEA 1994 (2 marks):** | Aspect | EU 2015/2283 | US DSHEA 1994 | |--------|--------------|---------------| | Pre-market authorisation | **REQUIRED** for all novel foods | NOT required; manufacturer responsibility | | Burden of proof | On applicant to demonstrate safety | FDA must prove harm | | Risk philosophy | Precautionary | Reactive | | New Dietary Ingredient (NDI) notification | Equivalent procedure for "supplement" category | Required only for NDI post-1994 | #### Model Answer — Part (b) The EU novel food authorisation procedure is a **multi-step centralised process** as follows: 1. **Step 1 — Pre-submission consultation (optional but recommended)** between the applicant and EFSA to clarify dossier requirements. 2. **Step 2 — Dossier submission** through the EC e-submission system. 3. **Step 3 — Initial completeness check** by Commission (1 month). 4. **Step 4 — EFSA scientific risk assessment** (9 months, extendable to 18 months). 5. **Step 5 — EFSA publishes scientific opinion** on the EC and EFSA websites. 6. **Step 6 — Risk management phase** — Commission proposes draft implementing act. 7. **Step 7 — SCoPAFF vote** (qualified majority) and European Parliament scrutiny (2 months). 8. **Step 8 — Authorisation** — food added to Union List (Annex I). 9. **Step 9 — 5-year data protection** for proprietary dossiers. The flowchart is presented in **Section 4.1 Diagram A** of these notes. *(Full credit for correctly drawing the flow with all 5 decision points and time annotations.)* *(Award 1 mark per major step, with 1 mark for the Union List outcome and 1 mark for data protection discussion.)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – Supplementary Pattern 2024]** **(a)** Discuss the **role of EFSA** in the EU regulatory framework for nutraceuticals and functional foods. Mention its **location, mandate, and key scientific panels**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **EU labelling requirements** for food supplements under Directive 2002/46/EC. What information is mandatory and what information is prohibited? **(7 marks)** #### Model Answer — Part (a) **Role of EFSA (3 marks):** EFSA (European Food Safety Authority) is the keystone of EU food safety. Established by **Regulation (EC) 178/2002**, it provides **independent scientific advice** to risk managers (European Commission, Parliament, Council). It does NOT authorise products; it only **assesses the safety**. **Location and Mandate (2 marks):** EFSA is headquartered in **Parma, Italy**. Its mandate covers food and feed safety, nutrition, animal health and welfare, plant protection, and emerging risks. **Key Scientific Panels Relevant to Nutraceuticals (2 marks):** - **NDA Panel** — Nutrition, Dietetic Products, and Allergies - **FEEDAP Panel** — Feed Additives and Products - **ANS Panel** — Food Additives and Nutrient Sources Added to Food - **CEF Panel** — Contaminants in the Food Chain - **EFSA Scientific Committee** — cross-cutting guidance #### Model Answer — Part (b) **Mandatory information under Directive 2002/46/EC Article 6 (4 marks):** 1. Name of the food category (e.g., "Food supplement with vitamins and minerals"). 2. Names of the vitamins/minerals present. 3. **Recommended daily dose** of the product. 4. Warning: *"Food supplements should not be used as a substitute for a varied diet."* 5. Warning: *"Keep out of the reach of young children."* 6. Statement: *"Do not exceed the stated recommended daily dose."* 7. Quantitative declaration of nutrient content per daily dose. **Prohibited information (1 mark):** - Any claim that the product can **prevent, treat, or cure human disease**. - Claims that a balanced diet cannot provide adequate nutrients. **Comparison with other regions (2 marks):** EU requires explicit warning labels whereas the **US permits structure-function claims** under DSHEA with disclaimer "This statement has not been evaluated by the FDA." --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Loss Triggers** > > 1. **Confusing NHCR 1924/2006 with Regulation 1169/2011** (FIC — Food Information to Consumers). NHCR governs **claims**; FIC governs **labelling in general**. > 2. **Using the old cut-off date 1997** for the Novel Food definition — must be specified as **15 May 1997**. > 3. **Forgetting that EFSA does not authorise products** — only assesses risk. The Commission authorises. > 4. **Missing the 5-year data protection** concept — it is a recurring board exam question. > 5. **Stating "low fat" without specifying the threshold** — under EU law, "low fat" = ≤ 3 g fat per 100 g (solids) or ≤ 1.5 g/100 ml (liquids). > 6. **Confusing NRV (EU) with RDA (US/Codex)** — NRV values differ slightly from US RDA values. --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST - ✅ EU operates a **pre-market authorisation** system; US operates **post-market surveillance**. - ✅ Three core claim types: **Nutrition, Health (Article 13), DRRC (Article 14)**. - ✅ Novel Food cut-off date: **15 May 1997**; Regulation **EU 2015/2283**. - ✅ EFSA: headquartered in **Parma, Italy**; **assessment only**, not authorisation. - ✅ Data protection period for novel food dossier: **5 years**. - ✅ Standard EFSA risk assessment window: **9 + 9 = 18 months max**. - ✅ Mandatory supplement warning: *"should not replace a varied diet."* - ✅ "Source of" claim threshold = **15 % NRV/100 g**; "High in" claim = **30 % NRV/100 g**. - ✅ Moringa, chia seeds, and UV-mushroom Vitamin D2 are classic **novel food** examples. - ✅ Union List = **Annex I** of Reg. 2015/2283 — the public register of authorised novel foods. - ✅ Article 10 of NHCR 1924/2006 enumerates **prohibited claims** (disease cure claims, weight-loss claims, harm-from-non-consumption claims). - ✅ **Reg. (EC) 178/2002** (General Food Law) is the foundational act; all food law descends from it. - ✅ SCOPAFF = Standing Committee on Plants, Animals, Food and Feed — risk management body. - ✅ Botanicals in food supplements fall under **national law**, not Directive 2002/46/EC. - ✅ **Disease Risk Reduction Claims (DRRCs)** under Article 14 require explicit EFSA approval + minimum consumption quantity disclosure. --- > **END OF MODULE 4 — EU GUIDELINES NOTES** > **Course:** PEFTT863 — Nutraceuticals and Functional Foods | **KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: FSSAI Guidelines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT863 - NUTRACEUTICALS AND FUNCTIONAL FOODS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** FSSAI Guidelines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of FSSAI Guidelines

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)** is the apex statutory body established under the **Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006 (FSS Act, 2006)**, which was operationalized with effect from **5th August 2011**. FSSAI operates under the administrative control of the **Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, Government of India**, and its mandate is to consolidate, regulate, and scientifically frame food safety norms across the entire Indian food processing and manufacturing ecosystem.

For nutraceuticals and functional foods, the governing regulation is specifically the **Food Safety and Standards (Health Supplements, Nutraceuticals, Food for Special Dietary Use, Food for Special Medical Purpose, Functional Food and Novel Food) Regulations, 2016** — commonly abbreviated as the **FSSAI Nutraceutical Regulations, 2016**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FSSAI Mission Statement (Official):** *"To ensure food safety and standards by regulating the manufacture, processing, distribution, sale and import of food so as to ensure safe and wholesome food for human consumption."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a country without traffic rules — every vehicle would drive wherever it wanted, speeds would be unchecked, and accidents would be inevitable. **FSSAI plays the exact same role for India's food industry that traffic rules play for vehicles on a highway.**

Let us break this analogy further:

| Real-World Concept | FSSAI Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Driving License | FSSAI License / Registration |
| Traffic Police | FSSAI Inspectors & Designated Officers |
| Highway Rules | FSSAI Regulations & Standards |
| Vehicle Fitness Certificate | Product Approval / NOC |
| Vehicle Number Plate | FSSAI Logo + License Number on Label |
| Speed Limits | Permissible Limits (Additives, Heavy Metals, etc.) |
| Accident Investigation | Food Testing & Recall Procedures |

In simpler terms, **without FSSAI, any manufacturer could claim anything on a label, use any ingredient, and sell to consumers with zero accountability.** FSSAI enforces accountability, traceability, and safety.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Statistic (Board Favorite):** FSSAI regulates approximately **$408 billion** worth of food products in India, impacting the health of **1.4 billion** citizens — making it one of the largest single food regulators in the world.

## 1.3 Historical Evolution of Food Regulation in India

The Indian food regulatory landscape evolved in three distinct phases:

1. **Pre-Independence Era (1940s):** Fragmented laws like the **Prevention of Food Adulteration Act (PFA), 1954**.
2. **Multiplicity Phase (1954–2006):** At least **8 different laws** governed food — including the Fruit Products Order (FPO), Meat Products Order, Milk & Milk Products Order, etc.
3. **Unified Phase (2006–Present):** The single umbrella **FSS Act, 2006**, which consolidated all previous legislation into one comprehensive code.

## 1.4 Statutory Hierarchy of FSSAI

The FSSAI ecosystem comprises the following tiers:

- **Central Government (MoHFW)** — Policy making & supersession authority
- **Food Authority (FSSAI Headquarters, New Delhi)** — Standards framing & enforcement
- **State Food Safety Commissioners** — State-level enforcement
- **Designated Officers (DO)** — District-level licensing authority
- **Food Safety Officers (FSO)** — Inspection & sampling officers
- **Adjudication & Tribunals** — Dispute resolution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Designated Officer (DO):** A person appointed by the State Government under **Section 36** of the FSS Act to grant licenses and enforce compliance within a designated area.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Food Safety Officer (FSO):** An officer appointed under **Section 37** of the FSS Act, empowered to inspect premises, draw samples, and investigate violations.

## 1.5 Definition of "Food" under the FSS Act

The FSS Act 2006 defines **food** in **Section 3(1)(j)** as:

> *“Food means any substance, whether processed, partially processed or unprocessed, which is intended for human consumption and includes primary food, genetically modified or engineered food, food supplements, nutraceuticals, functional food, food for special dietary use, food for special medical purpose, novel food, and any other substance which the Central Government may, by notification, specify as food.”*

This is a remarkably broad definition that explicitly includes nutraceuticals and functional foods within the legal definition of "food."

---

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Hook:** Examiners frequently ask: *"Enumerate the salient features of the FSS Act 2006"* or *"Discuss the role of FSSAI in regulating the nutraceutical industry."* Always begin such answers with the statutory definition and the 2016 Nutraceutical Regulations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix

## 2.1 Salient Features of the FSS Act, 2006

The FSS Act, 2006 is built on **10 foundational pillars**:

1. **Single Comprehensive Law** — Replaces **8 pre-existing food laws**.
2. **Risk-Based Approach** — Based on **Codex Alimentarius** (international food standards).
3. **Vertical & Horizontal Standards** — Both product-specific and additive-specific regulations.
4. **Mandatory Licensing** — **No entity can manufacture food without an FSSAI license.**
5. **Third-Party Audits & Self-Compliance** — Private sector participation in quality assurance.
6. **Right to Information** — Consumers can request information from Food Business Operators (FBOs).
7. **Penalty Provisions** — Fines up to **₹10 lakh** and imprisonment up to **life term** for serious violations (adulteration causing death).
8. **Recall Procedures** — Mandated for unsafe food products.
9. **Food Safety Management Systems (FSMS)** — Based on **HACCP / ISO 22000**.
10. **Special Provisions** — For nutraceuticals, GM foods, organic foods, and novel foods.

> [!NOTE]
> **Codex Alimentarius:** The international "Food Code" developed by FAO/WHO. FSSAI uses Codex standards as a baseline reference, then tailors them to Indian context.

## 2.2 Categories of FSSAI Licenses

Every Food Business Operator (FBO) in India must obtain one of the following:

| Category | Annual Turnover | Eligibility | License Validity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Registration (Basic)** | Up to ₹12 lakh | Petty retailers, hawkers, small cottage industries | 5 years |
| **State License** | ₹12 lakh to ₹20 crore | Mid-sized manufacturers, transporters, distributors | 5 years |
| **Central License** | Above ₹20 crore | Large manufacturers, importers, exporters, e-commerce operators | 5 years |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For nutraceutical and functional food manufacturing, manufacturers must obtain a **State License** (turnover < ₹20 crore) or **Central License** (turnover > ₹20 crore). Registration alone is **not permitted** for nutraceutical manufacturing.

## 2.3 FSSAI Nutraceutical Regulations 2016 — Deep Dive

The **Food Safety and Standards (Health Supplements, Nutraceuticals, Food for Special Dietary Use, Food for Special Medical Purpose, Functional Food and Novel Food) Regulations, 2016** is the cornerstone regulation. It was further amended in **2022** to tighten permissible claims.

### 2.3.1 Categories Defined under the 2016 Regulations

The regulation defines **6 distinct product categories**:

1. **Health Supplements (HS)**
2. **Nutraceuticals**
3. **Food for Special Dietary Use (FSDU)**
4. **Food for Special Medical Purpose (FSMP)**
5. **Functional Food**
6. **Novel Food**

### 2.3.2 Key Definitions (Board Critical)

> **Health Supplement (Regulation 2(1)(z)):** A product intended to supplement the normal diet, consisting of one or more dietary ingredients like vitamins, minerals, amino acids, herbs/botanicals, enzymes, or metabolites. Presented in dosage forms like capsules, tablets, liquids, powders.

> **Nutraceutical (Regulation 2(1)(zze)):** A product isolated or purified from food, marketed in a non-food matrix (tablets, capsules) at a concentration greater than its natural occurrence, intended to provide health benefits beyond basic nutrition.

> **Functional Food:** A food that has been fortified or enhanced with one or more bioactive substances to provide specific health benefits, while remaining in a normal food matrix.

> **Novel Food:** A food or ingredient that has no history of safe use as a food in India prior to 2007, requiring pre-market safety assessment.

### 2.3.3 Permitted Health Claims under FSSAI

| Claim Type | Example | Status |
|---|---|---|
| **Nutrient Function Claim** | "Vitamin C supports immunity" | Permitted |
| **Health Function Claim** | "Helps reduce cholesterol" | Permitted with evidence |
| **Disease Risk Reduction Claim** | "Reduces risk of osteoporosis" | Strictly regulated |
| **Structure Function Claim** | "Supports joint health" | Permitted with disclaimer |
| **Cure/Prevention Claims** | "Cures cancer" or "Prevents COVID-19" | **PROHIBITED** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Prohibited Claim (Board Exam Trap):** No food or nutraceutical can claim to **"cure," "diagnose," "treat," or "prevent" any disease.** Such claims make the product a **drug** under the Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940.

## 2.4 Labeling Requirements under FSSAI

The **Food Safety and Standards (Labelling and Display) Regulations, 2020** govern all labeling aspects. Every nutraceutical product label **must** contain:

| Mandatory Information | Example / Note |
|---|---|
| Product Name | Generic + Brand |
| FSSAI Logo + License Number | Mandatory |
| Net Quantity | g, ml, or number |
| Ingredients List | In descending order of quantity |
| Nutritional Information | Per 100g / per serving |
| Batch / Lot Number | For traceability |
| Date of Manufacture & Expiry | "Best before" for shelf-stable |
| Manufacturer Address | With PIN code |
| Country of Origin | Mandatory for imports |
| Veg / Non-Veg Symbol | Brown (veg) / Green (non-veg) |
| Allergen Declaration | Bold or underlined |
| Storage Conditions | If any special requirement |
| "NOT FOR MEDICINAL USE" disclaimer | Mandatory for nutraceuticals |
| Recommended Dosage | For health supplements |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Disclaimer for Nutraceuticals:** Labels **must** state *"NOT FOR MEDICINAL USE"* in bold capital letters — this is a regulatory safeguard to prevent misuse as pharmaceutical products.

## 2.5 Permissible Limits — Reference Matrix

The 2016 Regulations specify **maximum permissible limits** for bioactive substances. A few critical examples:

| Substance | Permissible Limit |
|---|---|
| Caffeine (in energy drinks) | ≤ 145 mg/L (now ≤ 200 mg/L after 2022 amendment for certain products) |
| Melatonin | ≤ 3 mg/day |
| Curcumin | ≤ 500 mg/day |
| Garlic Extract | ≤ 600 mg/day |
| Ginseng Extract | ≤ 200 mg/day |
| Heavy Metals (Lead) | ≤ 1.0 ppm (in supplements) |
| Heavy Metals (Arsenic) | ≤ 0.5 ppm |
| Heavy Metals (Cadmium) | ≤ 0.3 ppm |
| Heavy Metals (Mercury) | ≤ 0.1 ppm |
| Microbial Load (TPC) | ≤ 10⁵ CFU/g |

## 2.6 FSSAI Quick-Reference Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Since FSSAI Guidelines is a regulatory subject (not a quantitative engineering topic), the "formulas" here are procedural rules, limits, and structural formulae — each accompanied by its governing regulatory clause.

| # | Rule / Procedural Formula | Regulatory Source | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $TL < 12L \Rightarrow \text{Registration}$ | Sec 31(1) | Petty FBOs |
| 2 | $12L \leq TL \leq 20Cr \Rightarrow \text{State License}$ | Sec 31(2) | Mid-size FBOs |
| 3 | $TL > 20Cr \Rightarrow \text{Central License}$ | Sec 31(3) | Large FBOs |
| 4 | $\text{Nutraceutical Label} \ni \text{"NOT FOR MEDICINAL USE"}$ | Reg 5, 2016 | Mandatory disclaimer |
| 5 | $\text{Cure/Prevention Claims} = \text{PROHIBITED}$ | Reg 22, 2016 | Regulatory restriction |
| 6 | $L_{\text{Pb}} \leq 1.0 \text{ ppm}$ | Reg 4, 2016 | Heavy metal limit |
| 7 | $\text{Risk} = f(\text{Hazard} \times \text{Exposure})$ | Codex Risk Analysis | Risk assessment |
| 8 | $\text{MIC} = \frac{\text{Min Inhibitory Concentration}}{}$ | FSSAI Manual | Preservative efficacy |
| 9 | $N_{\text{label}} \geq 12 \text{ elements}$ | Label Reg 2020 | Labeling compliance |
| 10 | $\text{Validity of License} = 5 \text{ years}$ | Sec 32 | Licensing cycle |

> Where **TL** = Turnover in ₹, **MIC** = Minimum Inhibitory Concentration, **L_Pb** = Lead content limit.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework — Compliance Decision Tree

## 3.1 Analytical Decision Matrix — Is a Product Regulated by FSSAI Nutraceutical Regulations 2016?

In board exams, students are often asked to **classify a product** as Health Supplement, Nutraceutical, Functional Food, or Novel Food. The classification follows a strict **decision tree** that can be analytically derived.

### 3.1.1 The Classification Logic

Let us denote the product's characteristics by parameters:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Form} &= \begin{cases} \text{Dosage form (tablet, capsule, syrup)} & \Rightarrow \text{Not a regular food} \\ \text{Regular food matrix (beverage, snack)} & \Rightarrow \text{May be functional food} \end{cases}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Purpose} &= \begin{cases} \text{Supplement normal diet} & \Rightarrow \text{Health Supplement} \\ \text{Provide health benefits beyond nutrition} & \Rightarrow \text{Nutraceutical} \\ \text{Cater to specific dietary needs (athletes, infants)} & \Rightarrow \text{FSDU} \\ \text{Manage medical condition under supervision} & \Rightarrow \text{FSMP} \end{cases}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{History of Use} &= \begin{cases} \text{Used before 2007 in India} & \Rightarrow \text{Conventional ingredient} \\ \text{No history prior to 2007} & \Rightarrow \text{Novel Food — pre-market approval} \end{cases}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.1.2 Combined Classification Algorithm

The complete classification can be expressed as a Boolean function:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Category} = \begin{cases}
\text{HS} & \text{if Form = Dosage AND Purpose = Supplement} \\
\text{Nutraceutical} & \text{if Form = Dosage AND Purpose = Health Benefit AND Concentration} > \text{Natural} \\
\text{Functional Food} & \text{if Form = Food Matrix AND Purpose = Health Benefit} \\
\text{FSDU} & \text{if Purpose = Specific Dietary Need} \\
\text{FSMP} & \text{if Purpose = Medical Condition Management} \\
\text{Novel Food} & \text{if History = No prior 2007 use} \\
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Note:** If a product satisfies **multiple** criteria, the **most restrictive classification** applies. For example, a dosage-form product with both supplement and health-benefit claims is regulated as a **Nutraceutical**, the more stringent category.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Classification of Three Products

Let us classify three common products to demonstrate analytical application.

### Example 1: Omega-3 Fish Oil Soft Gel Capsules (1000 mg)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Form Analysis} & \rightarrow \text{Soft gel capsule} = \text{Dosage form} \\
\text{Step 2: Purpose Analysis} & \rightarrow \text{Label says "Supports heart health"} = \text{Health benefit} \\
\text{Step 3: Concentration Analysis} & \rightarrow \text{1000 mg} > \text{natural fish content} \\
\text{Step 4: History} & \rightarrow \text{Fish oil has pre-2007 use} \\
\text{Conclusion} & \rightarrow \text{NUTRACEUTICAL}
\end{aligned}$$

### Example 2: Probiotic Yogurt Drink with Added Lactobacillus

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Form Analysis} & \rightarrow \text{Yogurt drink} = \text{Regular food matrix} \\
\text{Step 2: Purpose Analysis} & \rightarrow \text{Improves gut health} = \text{Health benefit} \\
\text{Step 3: Concentration} & \rightarrow \text{Added probiotic culture} \\
\text{Conclusion} & \rightarrow \text{FUNCTIONAL FOOD}
\end{aligned}$$

### Example 3: Moringa Leaf Powder Tablets (500 mg)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Form Analysis} & \rightarrow \text{Tablet} = \text{Dosage form} \\
\text{Step 2: Purpose Analysis} & \rightarrow \text{Supplement normal diet with vitamins/minerals} \\
\text{Step 3: Concentration} & \rightarrow \text{Purified/isolated form} \\
\text{Conclusion} & \rightarrow \text{HEALTH SUPPLEMENT (if no specific health claim)} \\
& \quad \text{or NUTRACEUTICAL (if specific health claim made)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Insight:** The **distinction between Health Supplement and Nutraceutical** is the **single most tested concept** in Module 4. The distinguishing factor is the **purpose and claim**: HS supplements normal diet; Nutraceutical provides health benefits beyond nutrition at higher-than-natural concentrations.

## 3.3 Risk Assessment Analytical Framework

FSSAI follows a **structured risk assessment** approach. The risk to the consumer is computed using the standard food safety risk equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Risk} = \text{Hazard} \times \text{Exposure}
\end{aligned}$$

Where:

- **Hazard** = Inherent toxicity or adverse effect potential of the substance (qualitatively rated as Low, Medium, High)
- **Exposure** = Estimated daily intake based on consumption patterns (mg/kg body weight/day)

### 3.3.1 Risk Decision Outcomes

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Decision} = \begin{cases}
\text{Approve} & \text{if Risk} \leq \text{Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI)} \\
\text{Set Maximum Limit} & \text{if Risk slightly exceeds ADI} \\
\text{Reject} & \text{if Risk} \gg \text{ADI} \\
\text{Require Further Study} & \text{if Data is Insufficient}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}$$

> This framework is the basis for FSSAI's **Scientific Panel on Functional Foods, Nutraceuticals, and Dietary Supplements**, which evaluates every novel ingredient before approval.

## 3.4 Penalties and Enforcement Analysis

The penalty matrix under the FSS Act 2006 is highly structured:

| Violation Type | Section | Penalty |
|---|---|---|
| Selling substandard food | Sec 26 | Up to ₹5 lakh fine |
| Misbranded food | Sec 27 | Up to ₹3 lakh fine |
| Food containing extraneous matter | Sec 28 | Up to ₹1 lakh fine |
| Adulterated food (no injury) | Sec 26 | Up to 6 months imprisonment |
| Adulterated food (causing injury) | Sec 26 | Up to 1 year imprisonment |
| Adulterated food (causing death) | Sec 26 | Up to **life imprisonment** + ₹10 lakh fine |

> [!WARNING]
> **Misbranded vs. Adulterated:** A *misbranded* product has wrong/incorrect labels; an *adulterated* product contains harmful substances. Both are punishable, but adulteration carries far heavier penalties.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary — Why FSSAI Exists for Nutraceuticals

The regulatory need for FSSAI in the nutraceutical space arises from **three convergent market failures**:

1. **Information Asymmetry:** Consumers cannot independently verify health claims.
2. **Safety Risks:** High-dose bioactive compounds can cause toxicity (e.g., hypervitaminosis A from excessive retinoid supplements).
3. **Fraud Prevention:** Without regulations, adulteration with undeclared pharmaceuticals (sildenafil in "herbal" products, sibutramine in "weight loss" supplements) becomes rampant.

> **International Comparison:** The **US FDA** regulates nutraceuticals under **DSHEA (1994)** as dietary supplements. The **EU** uses the **EFSA** framework under **Regulation (EC) No 1924/2006** for nutrition and health claims. FSSAI's 2016 Regulations are broadly aligned with these international frameworks while addressing India's unique traditional medicine (AYUSH) heritage.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FSSAI Organizational Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Government of India Ministry of Health and Family Welfare MoHFW] --> B[Food Safety and Standards Authority of India FSSAI Headquarters New Delhi]
    B --> C[Scientific Panels 7 Expert Panels]
    B --> D[Central Advisory Committee]
    B --> E[State Food Safety Commissioners]
    E --> F[Designated Officers District Level Licensing]
    E --> G[Food Safety Officers Inspection and Sampling]
    F --> H[Food Business Operators FBOs]
    G --> H
    C --> I[Standards Framing]
    C --> J[Risk Assessment]
    C --> K[Product Approval]
```

> **Reading Note:** Scientific Panels are the **brains** of FSSAI. The Panel on *Functional Foods, Nutraceuticals, and Dietary Supplements* is directly relevant to this course.

## 4.2 Nutraceutical Product Classification Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Product Introduction] --> B{Product Form?}
    B -->|Dosage Form Tablet Capsule Syrup| C{Purpose?}
    B -->|Regular Food Matrix Beverage Snack| D{Fortified or Enhanced?}
    C -->|Supplement Normal Diet| E[Health Supplement HS]
    C -->|Health Benefit Beyond Nutrition| F[Nutraceutical]
    C -->|Specific Dietary Need| G[Food for Special Dietary Use FSDU]
    C -->|Medical Condition Management| H[Food for Special Medical Purpose FSMP]
    D -->|Yes with Added Bioactives| I[Functional Food]
    D -->|No| J[Regular Food]
    E --> K{History of Use in India Pre 2007?}
    F --> K
    G --> K
    H --> K
    I --> K
    K -->|Yes| L[Approved Ingredient List]
    K -->|No| M[Novel Food Requires Pre Market Approval]
```

## 4.3 FSSAI Licensing Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FBO Plans Food Business] --> B{Turnover TL}
    B -->|Less than 12 Lakh| C[FSSAI Registration Form A]
    B -->|12 Lakh to 20 Crore| D[State License Form B]
    B -->|More than 20 Crore| E[Central License Form B via FoSCoS]
    C --> F[Document Submission]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Designated Officer Inspection]
    G --> H{Compliance Status}
    H -->|Satisfactory| I[License Issued Validity 5 Years]
    H -->|Deficiencies| J[Rectification Period 30 Days]
    J --> G
    I --> K[License Number Display on All Labels]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — FSSAI Compliance Architecture for a Nutraceutical Manufacturer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Sourcing] --> B[Quality Testing at Incoming Bay]
    B --> C[Manufacturing under FSMS HACCP]
    C --> D[In Process Quality Control IPQC]
    D --> E[Finished Product Testing]
    E --> F{Compliance with FSSAI Standards?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Packaging and Labeling Compliance]
    F -->|No| H[Reject or Reprocess]
    G --> I[FSSAI Logo License Number Not for Medicinal Use Disclaimer]
    I --> J[Market Release]
    J --> K[Post Market Surveillance]
    K --> L{Adverse Event Reported?}
    L -->|No| M[Continue Sales]
    L -->|Yes| N[Voluntary or Mandatory Recall]
```

## 4.5 FSSAI Label Architecture (Mandatory Label Elements)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FSSAI Compliant Nutraceutical Label] --> B[Front Panel]
    A --> C[Back Panel]
    A --> D[Side Panels]
    B --> B1[Product Name]
    B --> B2[Brand Name]
    B --> B3[Net Quantity]
    B --> B4[Veg or Non Veg Symbol]
    B --> B5[FSSAI Logo and License Number]
    B --> B6[NOT FOR MEDICINAL USE Disclaimer]
    C --> C1[Ingredients List Descending Order]
    C --> C2[Nutritional Information]
    C --> C3[Allergen Declaration]
    C --> C4[Batch and Lot Number]
    C --> C5[Date of Manufacture and Expiry]
    C --> C6[Storage Conditions]
    D --> D1[Manufacturer Address with PIN]
    D --> D2[Country of Origin]
    D --> D3[Customer Care Details]
    D --> D4[MRP Inclusive of All Taxes]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The label has **three physical regions** (Front, Back, Side). The Front Panel is the **first visual contact** with the consumer and must display the **FSSAI logo and License Number prominently** — failure to do so is a punishable offense under Section 26 of the FSS Act.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**Q: Define FSSAI and list its primary objectives.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

FSSAI stands for **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India**, established under Section 4 of the **Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006** (FSS Act, 2006), operationalized on **5th August 2011**. It functions under the **Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, Government of India**.

**Primary Objectives:**

1. To frame science-based standards for articles of food (including nutraceuticals and functional foods).
2. To regulate manufacture, storage, distribution, sale, and import of food to ensure safe and wholesome food for human consumption.
3. To ensure consumer protection through labeling, packaging, and advertising regulations.

*(Award 1 mark for correct full form + statutory reference, 2 marks for objectives.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**Q: Differentiate between a Health Supplement and a Nutraceutical under the FSSAI 2016 Regulations.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Health Supplement | Nutraceutical |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | To supplement the normal diet with vitamins/minerals/amino acids | To provide health benefits beyond basic nutrition |
| **Concentration** | At or near natural levels | Higher than natural food concentration |
| **Form** | Capsules, tablets, syrups | Capsules, tablets, concentrates |
| **Examples** | Vitamin C tablets, Iron tablets | Omega-3 fish oil capsules, Curcumin capsules |

*(Award 1 mark for definition of HS, 1 mark for Nutraceutical, 1 mark for distinguishing criterion.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each module in the End Semester Examination (ESE) carries internal choice — students answer **ONE** of two alternatives. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each worth **7 marks**.

---

### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**Q: (a)** Discuss the salient features of the Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the FSSAI licensing system with reference to Food Business Operators (FBOs) involved in nutraceutical manufacturing. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — Salient Features of FSS Act, 2006 (7 Marks)

The Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006 is the **umbrella legislation** that replaced **8 different pre-existing food laws** (including the Prevention of Food Adulteration Act 1954, Fruit Products Order 1955, etc.). Its salient features are:

1. **Single Comprehensive Law (1 Mark):** Consolidates multiple food laws into one unified statute for better governance.

2. **Establishment of FSSAI (1 Mark):** Creates an apex statutory body under Section 4 with the mandate to frame and enforce food standards.

3. **Risk-Based Approach (1 Mark):** Standards are based on scientific risk assessment aligned with Codex Alimentarius principles.

4. **Mandatory Licensing (1 Mark):** All Food Business Operators (FBOs) must obtain FSSAI registration or license based on turnover thresholds.

5. **Penalty Provisions (1 Mark):** Prescribes graded penalties — fines up to **₹10 lakh** and imprisonment up to **life term** for adulteration causing death.

6. **Consumer Rights (1 Mark):** Right to information, recall procedures, and prohibition of misleading advertisements under Section 24.

7. **Special Provisions for Nutraceuticals (1 Mark):** Specific regulations (2016) for health supplements, nutraceuticals, functional foods, novel foods, FSDU, and FSMP.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — FSSAI Licensing for Nutraceutical Manufacturers (7 Marks)

Every Food Business Operator engaged in manufacturing, processing, packaging, or selling food products (including nutraceuticals) must obtain FSSAI authorization under **Section 31** of the FSS Act, 2006.

**Categories of Authorization (3 Marks):**

| Turnover | Authorization Type | Issuing Authority |
|---|---|---|
| Up to ₹12 lakh per annum | Registration (Form A) | Designated Officer |
| ₹12 lakh – ₹20 crore | State License (Form B) | State Food Safety Commissioner |
| Above ₹20 crore | Central License (Form B) | FSSAI HQ, New Delhi |

**Procedure for Obtaining License (2 Marks):**

1. Online application via **FoSCoS portal** (Food Safety and Standards System).
2. Document submission (ID proof, address proof, product list, layout plan, water analysis, recall plan).
3. Inspection by Designated Officer.
4. License issuance within **60 days** (or rejection with reasons).
5. **Validity: 5 years**, renewable.

**Specific Requirements for Nutraceutical Manufacturers (2 Marks):**

- Product approval from FSSAI Scientific Panel for each novel ingredient.
- Compliance with **Nutraceutical Regulations, 2016** (composition, labeling, claims).
- **"NOT FOR MEDICINAL USE"** disclaimer on every label.
- Implementation of **Food Safety Management System (FSMS)** based on HACCP.

---

### Question B [14 Marks] [Alternative Choice]

**Q: (a)** Define Nutraceuticals, Functional Foods, and Novel Foods as per FSSAI 2016 Regulations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the labeling requirements and permitted health claims for nutraceutical products in India under FSSAI. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — Definitions under FSSAI 2016 Regulations (7 Marks)

**1. Nutraceutical (Regulation 2(1)(zze)) — 2 Marks:**

A nutraceutical is a product isolated or purified from food, marketed in a **non-food matrix** (tablets, capsules, soft gels) at a concentration **greater than its natural occurrence** in food, intended to provide health benefits beyond basic nutrition. Examples: Curcumin capsules, Omega-3 fish oil soft gels, Lycopene capsules.

**2. Functional Food — 2 Marks:**

A functional food is a food product that is **fortified or enhanced** with one or more bioactive substances (probiotics, prebiotics, antioxidants) to provide specific health benefits, while **remaining in a normal food matrix** (yogurt, beverage, bread). Examples: Probiotic yogurt, fortified breakfast cereals, omega-3 enriched milk.

**3. Novel Food — 2 Marks:**

A novel food is a food or ingredient that has **no history of safe use as a food in India prior to 2007**. It requires **pre-market safety assessment** by FSSAI's Scientific Panel. Examples: Chia seeds (before 2016 inclusion), quinoa (until permitted), insect protein.

**Distinction — 1 Mark:**

The key distinction is **purpose, form, and novelty** — Nutraceutical is a dosage form providing health benefits; Functional Food is a regular food matrix enhanced with bioactives; Novel Food has no prior history and requires pre-market approval.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Labeling and Health Claims for Nutraceuticals (7 Marks)

**Labeling Requirements under FSSAI Labeling Regulations 2020 (4 Marks):**

Every nutraceutical product label must mandatorily display:

1. **Product Name and Brand**
2. **FSSAI Logo and License Number** (mandatory for traceability)
3. **Net Quantity** (in g, ml, or units)
4. **Ingredients List** in descending order of quantity
5. **Nutritional Information** (per 100g and per serving)
6. **Batch/Lot Number, Date of Manufacture, and Expiry**
7. **"NOT FOR MEDICINAL USE"** disclaimer (in bold capitals) — mandatory for all health supplements and nutraceuticals
8. **Allergen Declaration** (highlighted in bold)
9. **Veg/Non-Veg Symbol** (brown/green) — mandatory
10. **Recommended Dosage** and storage conditions

**Health Claims (3 Marks):**

| Permitted Claims | Prohibited Claims |
|---|---|
| Nutrient function: "Vitamin C supports immunity" | "Cures cancer" |
| Health function: "Helps maintain cholesterol levels" | "Prevents heart disease" |
| Structure-function: "Supports bone health" | "100% cure guaranteed" |
| Disease risk reduction (with evidence) | "Treats diabetes" |

> Any claim of **cure, diagnosis, treatment, or prevention** of disease automatically reclassifies the product as a **drug** under the Drugs & Cosmetics Act, 1940, and is **strictly prohibited**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on FSSAI Questions:**
> 1. **Missing "NOT FOR MEDICINAL USE" disclaimer** in label lists — students often omit this, losing **1 full mark**.
> 2. **Confusing turnover thresholds** — writing "₹10 lakh" instead of correct "₹12 lakh" for registration. Memorize: **12L → 20Cr**.
> 3. **Calling FSSAI a "Ministry"** — it is a **statutory body**, not a Ministry. Marks deducted.
> 4. **Confusing Health Supplement vs. Nutraceutical** — they are **not interchangeable**. Distinguish based on concentration and purpose.
> 5. **Writing "PFA Act"** — replaced in 2011 by FSS Act. Examiners treat this as outdated.
> 6. **Skipping the statutory section numbers** (Sec 31, Sec 26, etc.) — referencing the Act specifically earns higher marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 High-Yield Revision Points (must memorize before exam):**

- [x] FSSAI = Statutory body under **FSS Act, 2006**, operationalized **5 August 2011**, HQ **New Delhi**, under **Ministry of Health & Family Welfare**
- [x] **8 pre-existing laws** were consolidated by the FSS Act, 2006
- [x] **Turnover thresholds:** Registration (< ₹12L), State License (₹12L–₹20Cr), Central License (> ₹20Cr)
- [x] **License validity:** 5 years; **Issuance timeline:** within 60 days
- [x] **Three-tier enforcement:** FSSAI HQ → State Commissioner → Designated Officer + FSO
- [x] **Six product categories** under Nutraceutical Regulations 2016: HS, Nutraceutical, FSDU, FSMP, Functional Food, Novel Food
- [x] **Distinguishing HS vs. Nutraceutical:** Dosage form is common; difference is **purpose and concentration**
- [x] **Heavy metal limits:** Pb ≤ 1.0 ppm, As ≤ 0.5 ppm, Cd ≤ 0.3 ppm, Hg ≤ 0.1 ppm
- [x] **Mandatory label elements** (12 minimum): Name, Brand, FSSAI logo+license, Net Qty, Ingredients, Nutrition, Batch, Mfg/Expiry, Manufacturer address, Veg/Non-veg, Allergens, **"NOT FOR MEDICINAL USE"**
- [x] **Prohibited claims:** "Cure, diagnose, treat, prevent" any disease — reclassifies as drug
- [x] **Maximum penalty:** Life imprisonment + ₹10 lakh fine for adulteration causing death (Section 26)
- [x] **International alignment:** Codex Alimentarius (FAO/WHO); parallels US FDA (DSHEA) and EU EFSA frameworks
- [x] **Novel Food** = no history of use in India before 2007 — requires pre-market approval
- [x] **FSSAI portal:** FoSCoS (Food Safety Compliance System) for online licensing
- [x] **FSMS** = Food Safety Management System based on HACCP / ISO 22000 — mandatory for all licensed FBOs

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Strategy:** The most exam-worthy concepts in this module are: (1) **Licensing turnover thresholds**, (2) **Health Supplement vs. Nutraceutical distinction**, and (3) **Mandatory label elements**. These three topics alone account for **60-70%** of marks in any FSSAI-related question. Memorize them first.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT863 - NUTRACEUTICALS AND FUNCTIONAL FOODS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Regulations for Nutraceuticals and Functional Foods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🏛️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nutraceutical Regulation** refers to the comprehensive framework of laws, guidelines, standards, and administrative procedures established by governmental and quasi-governmental bodies to govern the **manufacture, labeling, marketing, safety assessment, and health claims** of nutraceuticals, dietary supplements, and functional foods. These regulations define what a product *is*, what it *can claim*, what it *must declare*, and what *evidence* must back its promises.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, the topic "Regulations for Nutraceuticals and Functional Foods" (Module 4 of PEFTT863) addresses the **statutory and voluntary frameworks** that convert a bioactive ingredient, herbal extract, or fortified food into a legally marketable commodity. The discipline sits at the intersection of **food law, pharmaceutical law, and consumer protection law**.

### Key Terminological Anchors

- **Nutraceutical** (Stephen DeFelice, 1989): "A food or part of a food that provides medical or health benefits, including the prevention and treatment of disease."
- **Functional Food** (FOSHU, Japan 1991): A food consumed as part of a normal diet but containing biologically active components that confer a demonstrated health benefit beyond basic nutrition.
- **Dietary Supplement** (DSHEA, USA 1994): A product intended to supplement the diet, containing one or more dietary ingredients (vitamins, minerals, herbs, amino acids), intended for ingestion in pill, capsule, tablet, or liquid form.
- **Health Claim**: Any statement that suggests a relationship between a food substance and a reduced risk of disease or health-related condition.
- **Structure-Function Claim**: A statement describing the role of a nutrient or dietary ingredient in maintaining normal structure/function in humans (e.g., "Calcium builds strong bones").
- **Nutrient Claim**: A claim that describes the level of a nutrient in a food (e.g., "High in fibre", "Low in fat").

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for Examiners:** The single most important regulatory distinction globally is between a **food-drug interface**. A product's *intended use* (its label claim) decides whether it is regulated as a food (FSSAI/FDA Food Code) or a drug (CDSCO/US-FDA Drug Division). The same molecule can be either depending on wording — this is the foundation of nutraceutical law.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Driving Licence" Model 🚗

Imagine nutraceutical regulations as a **driving licence system for molecules**:

| Analogy Element | Real-World Regulatory Equivalent |
|---|---|
| A person wanting to drive | A bioactive compound seeking market entry |
| Traffic Rules & RTO | FSSAI / FDA / EFSA Regulations |
| Driving Licence | Product Approval / License Number (FSSAI Logo + License No.) |
| Speed Limit signboards | Permitted Nutrient & Health Claims |
| RTO Inspection (fitness test) | Safety Assessment & Toxicity Studies |
| Honking rules (no nuisance) | Labeling & Advertising Code |
| Penalty for violating rules | Product Recall, Fines, License Cancellation |

Just as you cannot legally drive without a licence and cannot put random signs on the road, you cannot market a nutraceutical without regulatory clearance, and you cannot write *any* claim you wish. **The molecule is innocent until its claims prove it guilty — but the burden of proof lies with the manufacturer, not the regulator.**

---

## 1.3 Why Regulations Exist — The Core "Why"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Pillars of Nutraceutical Regulation:**
> 1. **Consumer Safety** — Protect against adulteration, contamination, toxic doses, drug interactions
> 2. **Truth in Labeling** — Prevent misleading or fraudulent health claims
> 3. **Fair Trade** — Establish a level playing field for legitimate manufacturers

A regulatory vacuum (as existed in India before 2006 FSSAI Act) leads to:
- Adulteration with pharmaceutical drugs (e.g., sildenafil in "herbal" aphrodisiacs)
- False cancer-cure claims on turmeric capsules
- Heavy metal contamination in Ayurvedic exports
- Untraceable supply chains

---

## 1.4 Major Regulatory Bodies — A Snapshot

| Region / Country | Primary Authority | Governing Act / Framework |
|---|---|---|
| 🇮🇳 India | **FSSAI** (Food Safety & Standards Authority of India) | Food Safety & Standards Act, 2006; FSSAI Nutraceutical Regulations 2016, FSSR 2022 |
| 🇺🇸 United States | **FDA** (Food & Drug Administration) | FD&C Act 1938; DSHEA 1994; FSMA 2011 |
| 🇪🇺 European Union | **EFSA** (European Food Safety Authority) | EU Regulation 1924/2006 (Nutrition & Health Claims) |
| 🇯🇵 Japan | **CAA** (Consumer Affairs Agency) | FOSHU System, Foods with Function Claims (FFC) 2015 |
| 🇨🇳 China | **SAMR** (State Administration for Market Regulation) | Food Safety Law 2015; Health Food Regulation 2016 |
| 🌍 Global Codex | **Codex Alimentarius Commission (FAO/WHO)** | Codex Guidelines for Vitamin & Mineral Supplements CAC/GL 55-2005 |
| 🌐 Harmonization | **WHO**, **UNCTAD**, **ISO 22000** | International standards |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧠 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 The Global Regulatory Architecture — Conceptual Decomposition

Regulatory frameworks for nutraceuticals cluster around **three dominant philosophical models**:

### Model A — The "Food-First" Model (EU, India, Japan)
- Nutraceuticals are legally **foods**, not drugs.
- Health claims require **pre-market scientific assessment** by an independent body (EFSA, FSSAI Scientific Panel).
- Burden of proof: **High** — randomized controlled trials (RCTs) required for novel claims.
- Result: Conservative, evidence-anchored market.

### Model B — The "Supplement-First" Model (USA — DSHEA)
- Dietary supplements are a **sub-category of food**.
- Manufacturer is responsible for safety; FDA does **not** pre-approve.
- Claims (structure-function) allowed **without FDA approval**, but with mandatory disclaimer.
- Burden of proof: **Lower at launch**; FDA must prove harm to remove.
- Result: Larger market, more recalls.

### Model C — The "Traditional-Medicine Bridge" Model (China, India Ayurveda)
- Traditional/herbal products can use classical texts as evidence.
- China: "Health Foods" require SFDA registration.
- India: ASU (Ayurveda, Siddha, Unani) products governed by Ministry of AYUSH + FSSAI for food categories.

---

## 2.2 🇮🇳 Indian Regulatory Framework — FSSAI in Depth

### 2.2.1 Statutory Foundation
The **Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006** (replaced the earlier Prevention of Food Adulteration Act 1954) is the **mother statute**. The operative regulations appear in:
- **Food Safety and Standards Rules, 2011**
- **Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011**
- **Food Safety and Standards (Health Supplements, Nutraceuticals, Food for Special Dietary Use, Food for Special Medical Purpose, Functional Food and Novel Food) Regulations, 2016** — the *core* nutraceutical regulation in India.
- **Food Safety and Standards (Advertising and Claims) Regulations, 2018**

### 2.2.2 FSSAI 2016 Regulation — Product Categories

| Category | Definition Example |
|---|---|
| **Health Supplements** | Vitamins, minerals, amino acids in dosage form |
| **Nutraceuticals** | Isolated/concentrated bioactive from food (e.g., omega-3 capsules) |
| **Food for Special Dietary Use (FSDU)** | Foods for sportspersons, infants, pregnant women |
| **Food for Special Medical Purpose (FSMP)** | Foods for patients under medical supervision (e.g., renal diets) |
| **Functional Food** | Conventional food with added bioactive (e.g., probiotic yogurt) |
| **Novel Food** | Food not used to a significant degree before 2007 (e.g., chia seeds initially) |

### 2.2.3 The FSSAI Licensing Process

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FSSAI License Number Format:**
> - **Basic Registration** (turnover < ₹12 lakh): 14-digit number, e.g., `12345678901234`
> - **State License** (₹12 lakh – ₹20 cr): Display on label with logo
> - **Central License** (> ₹20 cr, or specific categories): Mandatory for 100% EOU, importers

### 2.2.4 Permitted Claims under FSSAI

| Claim Type | FSSAI Stance |
|---|---|
| **Nutrient Content Claims** | Permitted with defined thresholds (e.g., "Low sodium" ≤ 120 mg/100 g) |
| **Health Claims** | Only those specifically approved by FSSAI scientific panel |
| **Disease Risk Reduction** | Generally **prohibited** for non-drug food products |
| **"Cures/Prevents/Treats"** | Strictly prohibited — becomes drug claim |
| **Comparative claims** | Permitted if substantiated |

---

## 2.3 🇺🇸 United States — DSHEA Framework

### 2.3.1 The DSHEA Watershed (1994)
The **Dietary Supplement Health and Education Act of 1994** fundamentally redefined the landscape:

- Dietary supplements were placed under the *general food safety umbrella*, **not** as drugs.
- Manufacturers **do not need FDA approval** before marketing (with one exception: new dietary ingredients, NDI, post-1994).
- Manufacturer must ensure product safety and label accuracy.
- FDA bears the burden of proving a product is **unsafe** to remove it.
- **Mandatory disclaimer** on structure-function claims:
  > *"This statement has not been evaluated by the Food and Drug Administration. This product is not intended to diagnose, treat, cure, or prevent any disease."*

### 2.3.2 Categories of Claims (21 CFR 101)

| Claim Type | Example | Pre-Market Approval? |
|---|---|---|
| **Nutrient Content Claim** | "Good source of Vitamin C (10% DV)" | No |
| **Structure-Function Claim** | "Supports immune health" | No (disclaimer + notification to FDA within 30 days) |
| **Health Claim (Authoritative)** | "Calcium may reduce osteoporosis risk" | Yes — FDA-authorized after scientific review |
| **Qualified Health Claim** | "Some evidence suggests selenium may reduce cancer risk" | Yes — with disclaimer |
| **Medical Claim** | "Treats diabetes" | **PROHIBITED** — turns product into a drug |

### 2.3.3 DSHEA Notifications — The 30-Day Rule
A manufacturer using a structure-function claim must notify FDA **within 30 days of marketing**, including the text of the claim and the name/address of the responsible firm.

---

## 2.4 🇪🇺 European Union — EFSA Regime

### 2.4.1 Regulation (EC) No 1924/2006
This is the **cornerstone** of EU nutrition and health claim law. It establishes:

- **Article 13.1 Claims**: Well-established function claims (e.g., "Vitamin C contributes to normal immune function") based on generally accepted scientific evidence.
- **Article 13.5 Claims**: New, novel claims based on emerging science, requiring proprietary data.
- **Article 14 Claims**: Disease risk reduction claims — e.g., "Plant sterols lower blood cholesterol, reducing CVD risk" — require **very high** scientific substantiation (human intervention studies).

### 2.4.2 The Nutrient Profile Criterion
EFSA mandated (and controversially applied in 2009) a **nutrient profile** for foods carrying claims — to prevent a high-sugar cereal from claiming "high in fibre." Implementation has been postponed multiple times.

### 2.4.3 Novel Food Regulation (EU 2015/2283)
Covers foods not used to a significant degree before **May 1997** in the EU. Requires pre-market authorization based on safety assessment by EFSA.

---

## 2.5 🇯🇵 Japan — FOSHU & Foods with Function Claims (FFC)

- **FOSHU (Foods for Specified Health Uses)** since 1991 — pre-market approval by Consumer Affairs Agency. Requires clinical evidence.
- **FFC (Foods with Function Claims)** since 2015 — **self-reported** system; manufacturer files notification with scientific evidence; no pre-market approval. Closer to US DSHEA in philosophy.

---

> Below is the **exam-oriented cheat sheet** capturing every regulation, threshold, and key number you must memorize.

| Parameter | Value / Rule | Source |
|---|---|---|
| FSSAI Act year | **2006** | Statute |
| Nutraceutical specific regulation year | **2016** | FSSAI Notification |
| DSHEA enactment (USA) | **1994** | Public Law 103-417 |
| EU Health Claims Regulation | **EC 1924/2006** | Official Journal L 404 |
| FSSAI Basic Registration turnover ceiling | **< ₹12 lakh/yr** | FSSAI 2011 Rules |
| FSSAI Central License turnover | **> ₹20 cr/yr** | FSSAI 2011 Rules |
| FSSAI License Validity | **1–5 years** (renewable) | FSSAI Rules |
| DSHEA Notification to FDA for structure-function claim | **30 days** post-marketing | 21 CFR 101.93 |
| EU Novel Food baseline date | **15 May 1997** | EU 2015/2283 |
| FOSHU introduction year | **1991** | Japan MHLW |
| Japan FFC introduction year | **2015** | CAA Notification |
| China Food Safety Law | **2015** | NPC Standing Committee |
| Codex Vitamin/Mineral Guidelines | **CAC/GL 55-2005** | Codex Alimentarius |
| Nutrient claim "Low Sodium" threshold | **≤ 120 mg/100 g** | FSSAI/FDA |
| Nutrient claim "Reduced" minimum reduction | **≥ 25%** vs reference | FSSAI 2018 Claims Reg. |
| ADI acceptable daily intake | **mg/kg body weight/day** | JECFA / EFSA |
| Recommended Dietary Allowance (RDA) basis | **EAR + 2 SD** | ICMR/NCM |
| Health claim scientific evidence standard | **Randomized Controlled Trials (RCTs)** | EFSA/FSSAI Panel |

### Critical Conversions & Standards

$$ \text{Permissible Upper Intake Level (UL)} = \text{No Observed Adverse Effect Level (NOAEL)} \times \text{Uncertainty Factor (UF)} $$

$$ \text{UF} = 10 \text{ (animal to human)} \times 10 \text{ (intra-human variability)} = 100 \text{ typically} $$

$$ \text{Nutrient Reference Value (NRV) example — Vitamin C} = 40 \text{ mg/day (FSSAI)} $$

$$ \text{% DV (Daily Value)} = \frac{\text{Nutrient per serving}}{\text{RDA}} \times 100 $$

This conceptual transferability is why **regulatory literacy is a high-employment skill** in the food processing, pharma, and biotech industries.
